before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes win Centennial Cup championship with 5-2 victory over Humboldt Broncos.
before.descriptionLong: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup Tier Two junior-A hockey championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on May 10, 1987. Goaltender Frank Romeo earned MVP honours in the four-team roundrobin series, stopping 46 shots. Paul Rutherford and Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond in the winning effort.
before.headline: Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup Championship

after.descriptionShort: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup Tier Two junior-A hockey championship, with goaltender Frank Romeo named tournament MVP after stopping 46 shots.
after.descriptionLong: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup Tier Two junior-A hockey championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Goaltender Frank Romeo was named most valuable player of the four-team round-robin series after turning aside 46 shots while Richmond managed only 23 on the Broncos' net. Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third period and Dave Tomlinson also scored for Richmond; Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke replied for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos.
after.headline: Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup Championship

issues: The draft says the game was played on May 10, 1987, but the OCR says 'Saturday night' and the paper is dated May 11, 1987 — the actual game date per the Centennial Cup final was May 9, 1987 (Saturday), not May 10.

The draft says 'stopping 46 shots' for Romeo but the OCR says he 'stood up to 46 Humboldt shots' while Richmond managed only 23 — this phrasing is acceptable, but the draft omits that Richmond only generated 23 shots, a notable detail.
The draft says Rutherford scored but the OCR specifies he 'scored the winning goal in the third' — the draft's characterisation is not wrong but undersells the detail.

The headline 'Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup Championship' does not match any clear headline in the OCR; there is no distinct headline visible — it appears to be a wire brief without a standalone headline.

before.descriptionShort: Dave Tomlinson signs one-year contract with German Elite League's Mannheim after two seasons as Cincinnati Cyclones' leading scorer.
before.descriptionLong: Cincinnati Cyclones center Dave Tomlinson, the team's leading scorer for two seasons, signed a one-year contract with Mannheim of Germany's Elite League on June 15, 1996. The deal more than doubles his previous Cyclones salary of under $100,000. Tomlinson sought compensation in the top three of the league but accepted Mannheim's offer after contract extension negotiations with Cincinnati failed.
before.headline: Cyclones center Tomlinson signs with German team

after.descriptionShort: Ex-Sockeyes center Dave Tomlinson signs a one-year deal with Mannheim of Germany's Elite League after two seasons as Cincinnati Cyclones' leading scorer; a sidebar notes Cyclones coach Ron Smith is a candidate for the Toronto Maple Leafs head coaching job.
after.descriptionLong: Cincinnati Cyclones center Dave Tomlinson, former Richmond Sockeyes player and the team's leading scorer for two seasons, signed a one-year contract with Mannheim of Germany's Elite League on June 15, 1996, for more than double his Cyclones salary believed to be just under $100,000. Tomlinson, 28, had sought around $140,000 but negotiations with Cincinnati broke down, prompting him to accept Mannheim's offer; Mannheim also provides additional benefits beyond salary. The article also notes that Tomlinson played 31 games with the Winnipeg Jets (NHL), and a sidebar reports that Cyclones coach Ron Smith is a leading candidate to replace Nick Beverley as head coach of the Toronto Maple Leafs.
after.headline: Cyclones center Tomlinson signs with German team

issues: The draft's headline and description omit the second major story on the page: that Cyclones coach Ron Smith is a candidate for the Toronto Maple Leafs head coaching job, which receives roughly equal column space in the OCR.

The headline in the draft ('Cyclones center Tomlinson signs with German team') is plausible but the OCR does not clearly show a single headline — the two stories appear to be side-by-side items, so the drafted headline is an editorial inference rather than confirmed OCR text.

The long description says Tomlinson 'sought compensation in the top three of the league' — more precisely, he wanted to be paid 'somewhere among the top three in the league' (~$140,000), which the draft captures adequately, but it omits that Mannheim also provides housing/benefits (OCR: 'Mannheim will also provide Tomlinson')—the sentence is cut off but implies additional compensation beyond salary.
The draft does not mention that Tomlinson played 31 games with the Winnipeg Jets of the NHL, which is relevant context in the article.
The byline 'Tim Pennington' is correctly identified as 'BY TIM PENNINGTON' but his credit is 'Enquirer contributor', not a staff writer — a minor but factual nuance.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes scored three third-period goals to defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 in Centennial Cup roundrobin play, improving to 2-0 in the tournament.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes beat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 on May 4, 1987, in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, during the Centennial Cup junior-A championship tournament, with three goals coming in the third period. Dave Tomlinson scored twice, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek added one each; Czenczek's goal at 6:35 of the third period proved to be the winner. The victory gave Richmond a 2-0 roundrobin record and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, the Humboldt Broncos. Pembroke Lumber Kings (Ontario) and Dartmouth Fuel Kids fell to 0-2.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win 4-1 Over Pembroke in Centennial Cup Tournament

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes scored three third-period goals to defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 in Centennial Cup roundrobin play, improving to 2-0 in the tournament.
after.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes beat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 on May 4, 1987, in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, during the Centennial Cup junior-A championship tournament, with three goals coming in the third period. The victory gave Richmond a 2-0 roundrobin record and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, the Humboldt Broncos. Pembroke Lumber Kings (Ontario) and Dartmouth Fuel Kids fell to 0-2.
after.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win 4-1 Over Pembroke in Centennial Cup Tournament

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in Centennial Cup semifinal, advance to final.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, from British Columbia, defeated the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup Junior A semifinal on Thursday night in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Richmond advanced to the final game with a 2-1 record in the roundrobin portion of the tournament and will face the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in a sudden-death final on Saturday night.
before.headline: Sockeyes Whip Lumber Kings in Centennial Cup Semifinal

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup Junior A semifinal in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, advancing to the final against the Humboldt Broncos.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup Junior A semifinal on Thursday night in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Richmond, which advanced from round-robin play with a 2-1 record, will face the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the sudden-death final on Saturday night. Goals were scored by Dean Rutledge, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Mike McCormick, and Bill Hardy. The article is a Canadian Press wire report with no byline or headline visible in the OCR.
after.headline: Sockeyes Whip Lumber Kings in Centennial Cup Semifinal

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes scored three third-period goals to defeat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1, moving to 2-0 in Centennial Cup round-robin play in Humboldt, Saskatchewan.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, BC champions, exploded for three goals in the third period to defeat the Ontario-representing Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior-A championship tournament in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. The win gave the Sockeyes a 2-0 round-robin record, tying them for first place with Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. The Lumber Kings fell to 0-2, while the Dartmouth Fuel Kids also remained winless at 0-2. The tournament's second- and third-place finishers after round-robin play were scheduled to meet in a semifinal on Thursday, with the winner advancing to face the first-place team.
before.headline: Sockeyes move to 2-0

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes scored three third-period goals to defeat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1, moving to 2-0 in Centennial Cup round-robin play in Humboldt, Saskatchewan.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, BC champions, exploded for three goals in the third period to defeat the Ontario-representing Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior-A championship tournament in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. The win gave the Sockeyes a 2-0 round-robin record, tying them for first place with Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. The Lumber Kings fell to 0-2, while the Dartmouth Fuel Kids also remained winless at 0-2. The tournament's second- and third-place finishers after round-robin play were scheduled to meet in a semifinal on Thursday, with the winner advancing to face the first-place team.
after.headline: Sockeyes move to 2-0

before.descriptionShort: Article on Winnipeg Jets' AHL franchise arrangement with Moncton Hawks and roster updates for upcoming season.
before.descriptionLong: The Times-Transcript reports on the Winnipeg Jets' continued commitment to their AHL franchise in Moncton for the 1988-89 season. Jets general manager John Ferguson expresses interest in a long-term deal, while Hawks ownership remains cautious after the previous season's poor performance. Coach Rick Bowness promises a competitive team. The article notes nine players, including Matt Hervey, Ron Wilson, and Bob Essansa, were assigned to Moncton following the Jets' exhibition game against Minnesota.
before.headline: Moncton Hawks, Winnipeg Jets Explore Long-Term Arrangement

after.descriptionShort: Times-Transcript article on the Winnipeg Jets–Moncton Hawks AHL arrangement for 1988-89, noting that ex-Sockeye Matt Hervey was among nine players assigned to Moncton after a Jets exhibition game against Minnesota.
after.descriptionLong: The Times-Transcript reports on the uncertain future of the Winnipeg Jets' AHL affiliation with the Moncton Hawks as the 1988-89 season begins. Jets GM John Ferguson says he wants a long-term deal, but Hawks ownership is cautious after the previous season's poor on-ice performance and attendance drop. Coach Rick Bowness pledges a more competitive roster. Notably, nine players were assigned to Moncton following Winnipeg's exhibition game against Minnesota, including ex-Richmond Sockeyes player Matt Hervey, along with Ron Wilson, Brent Hughes, Guy Larose, Len Nielsen, Todd Flichel, Jamie Husgen, and goaltenders Stephane Beauregard and Bob Essensa.
after.headline: Moncton Hawks, Winnipeg Jets Explore Long-Term Arrangement

before.descriptionShort: Box score and game recap of Moncton vs. Nova Scotia junior hockey match from January 4, 1988.
before.descriptionLong: The Times-Transcript reports on a junior hockey game between Moncton and Nova Scotia played on January 4, 1988. Moncton defeated Nova Scotia 4–1, with goals from Matt Hervey, Guy Larose, Ron Pesetti, and Peter Douris. Goaltender Bob Essensa recorded the win for Moncton, while Warren Skorodenski played for Nova Scotia. The page also includes coverage of a Canada vs. Soviet Union exhibition game and World Junior Championship standings from Moscow.

after.descriptionShort: Box score and game recap of a Moncton 4–1 win over Nova Scotia in junior hockey, January 4, 1988, featuring ex-Sockeyes player Matt Hervey scoring the opening goal and earning a second-star selection.
after.descriptionLong: The Times-Transcript reports on a junior hockey game in which Moncton defeated Nova Scotia 4–1. Former Richmond Sockeyes player Matt Hervey scored the first goal (a power-play marker at 2:13 of the first period) and was named second star of the game. Guy Larose, Ron Pesetti, and Peter Douris added goals for Moncton, while goaltender Bob Essensa (6-6-0) earned the win over Nova Scotia's Warren Skorodenski. The page also includes box scores for a Canada vs. Soviet Union exhibition game (Canada 5, Soviet Union 2) and World Junior Championship standings from Moscow.

before.descriptionShort: A Times-Transcript column reports that Winnipeg Jets GM John Ferguson has been convinced by Moncton Hawks coach Rick Bowness to re-sign tough defenceman Matt Hervey, a former 1987 Richmond Sockeyes player.
before.descriptionLong: A Times-Transcript hockey notes column from April 12, 1988 reports that Moncton Hawks coach Rick Bowness has persuaded Jets GM John Ferguson to re-sign defenceman Matt Hervey — a former 1987 Richmond Sockeyes standout — for the following season. The column also covers Bowness's plans to add veteran Ron Wilson to the Moncton AHL roster as both a player (40–50 games) and an assistant to Bowness, noting Wilson's strong AHL track record. Additional notes discuss Joel Baillargeon being told not to return to training camp, Randy Gilhen's NHL prospects, and the possibility of Dwight Schofield and Gilles Hamel joining Moncton next season.

before.descriptionShort: Moncton Hawks defeat Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4 in American Hockey League play with converted defensemen Matt Hervey and Jamie Husgen contributing to third-period rally.
before.descriptionLong: The Moncton Hawks moved into third place in the AHL's Northern Division by defeating the Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4 on October 28, 1987. Coach Rick Bowness converted defensemen Matt Hervey and Jamie Husgen to right wing positions, and their aggressive play sparked a five-goal third-period comeback. Hervey scored twice and Jamie Gilhen added two goals in the comeback effort before 1,799 fans at the Baltimore Arena.
before.headline: Moncton Hawks Rally Past Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4

after.descriptionShort: Moncton Hawks defeat Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4 in AHL play, with ex-Sockeyes defenseman Matt Hervey converted to right wing and contributing to a five-goal third-period rally.
after.descriptionLong: The Moncton Hawks moved into third place in the AHL's Northern Division by defeating the Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4 on October 28, 1987, before 1,799 fans at Baltimore Arena. Coach Rick Bowness converted defensemen Matt Hervey (ex-Richmond Sockeyes) and Jamie Husgen to right wing, and their hard-hitting play sparked a five-goal third-period comeback after the Hawks trailed 4-2. Randy Gilhen scored twice in the third, including the winner at 15:43, with Chris Levasseur adding an insurance goal; Hervey scored once to cut the early deficit.
after.headline: Moncton Hawks Rally Past Baltimore Skipjacks 6-4

before.descriptionShort: Opinion column criticizing Winnipeg Jets' management for tolerating goaltender Daniel Berthiaume's insubordination and absence.
before.descriptionLong: An opinion piece from The Times-Transcript examines the Winnipeg Jets' handling of troubled goaltender Daniel Berthiaume, who has twice refused to report to the AHL's Moncton Hawks. The column argues the Jets should trade Berthiaume rather than accept his disruptive behavior, noting interim GM Mike Smith's hesitation despite the team's other goaltending prospects. The article contrasts Berthiaume's conduct with veteran Gilles Hamel's professionalism.
before.headline: Jets show too much patience with Bandit

after.descriptionShort: Notes item in a Moncton Hawks column reports that ex-Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey resumed skating after missing three games with a leg injury, leading the Hawks in plus-minus statistics.
after.descriptionLong: A hockey notes column in The Times-Transcript includes an item on former Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey, then with the AHL's Moncton Hawks, who returned to practice on Monday after missing three games with a leg injury. Coach Rick Bowness hoped the second-year pro would be back in the lineup by the weekend. Hervey was leading the Hawks in plus-minus statistics at the time. The broader column covers Winnipeg Jets goaltender Daniel Berthiaume's repeated refusal to report to the Hawks and argues the Jets should trade him rather than tolerate his insubordination.
after.headline: Jets show too much patience with Bandit

before.descriptionShort: Moncton Hawks prepare for Atlantic Division playoff series against St. John's Maple Leafs, emphasizing defensive strategy.
before.descriptionLong: The Moncton Hawks face the first-place St. John's Maple Leafs in an Atlantic Division playoff semifinal beginning Wednesday night in Halifax. Hawks coach Rob Laird emphasizes team defence and the effectiveness of their checking line—Rob Murray, Russ Romaniuk, and Alan Kerr, known as "The Blanket"—while acknowledging St. John's depth and balanced scoring threats from defencemen Jeff Serowik and Ted Crowley.
before.headline: Hawks ready for Leafs challenge

after.descriptionShort: Ex-Sockeye Dave Tomlinson, skating on the St. John's Maple Leafs' top AHL line with Kent Manderville and Patrik Augusta, is highlighted as an offensive threat ahead of the Atlantic Division semifinal against the Moncton Hawks.
after.descriptionLong: A 1993 AHL playoff preview in the Times-Transcript notes that ex-Richmond Sockeye Dave Tomlinson has joined Kent Manderville and Patrik Augusta on the St. John's Maple Leafs' top line, with the trio combining for 12 points and a +12 rating in a recent game against Halifax. The article previews the best-of-seven Atlantic Division semifinal between the Moncton Hawks and first-place St. John's, beginning Wednesday in Halifax. Moncton coach Rob Laird acknowledges the difficulty of matching lines against St. John's balanced attack, citing their checking line "The Blanket" (Rob Murray, Russ Romaniuk, Alan Kerr) as key, while the author predicts St. John's in five games.
after.headline: Hawks ready for Leafs challenge

before.descriptionLong: A "Hawks, Leafs at a glance" sidebar in The Times-Transcript previews the best-of-seven AHL Atlantic Division semifinal between the Moncton Hawks and St. John's Maple Leafs, with Game 1 set for the Halifax Metro Centre on April 14, 1993. The sidebar highlights that former Richmond Sockeye Dave Tomlinson of the St. John's Maple Leafs led the entire AHL with six shorthanded goals during the regular season. An accompanying article features Hawks captain Rob Murray and coach Rob Laird embracing the underdog label ahead of the series, with the Leafs playing home games in Halifax due to a municipal workers' strike in St. John's.

before.descriptionShort: Profile of Moncton Hawks centre and ex-Richmond Sockeyes junior Dave Tomlinson, whom Hawks coach Rob Laird calls the best two-way forward in the AHL, leading the team with 11 goals in 12 games.
before.descriptionLong: Dave Tomlinson, 24, a centre for the AHL's Moncton Hawks and former Tier II junior player in Richmond, B.C. with the Sockeyes, is profiled ahead of a game in Fredericton. Hawks coach Rob Laird calls him the best two-way forward in the AHL, praising his penalty-killing and defensive tenacity alongside his league-leading offensive pace of 11 goals in 12 games. Tomlinson credits his defensive development to Boston University coach Jackie Parker, and was the third overall pick in the 1989 supplemental draft by the Toronto Maple Leafs; he turned pro with the AHL's St. John's Maple Leafs before being dealt to the Florida Panthers and ultimately the Winnipeg Jets organization, which assigned him to Moncton.
before.headline: Tomlinson makes smooth transition to two-way forward

before.descriptionShort: Moncton Hawks defeat PEI Senators 4-1 in AHL game; Dave Tomlinson returns from Winnipeg Jets stint.
before.descriptionLong: The Moncton Hawks defeated the Prince Edward Island Senators 4-1 in an American Hockey League game at the Coliseum before 2,650 spectators. Hawks forward Dave Tomlinson returned to the lineup after a 23-game stint with the NHL's Winnipeg Jets. The win improves Moncton's record to 31-26-3 and solidifies their hold on second place in the Atlantic Division, trailing St. John's by eight points.
before.headline: Hawks solidify hold on second place

after.descriptionShort: Former Sockeye Dave Tomlinson returns to the Moncton Hawks lineup after a 23-game NHL stint with the Winnipeg Jets, as the Hawks defeat the PEI Senators 4-1 before 2,650 fans.
after.descriptionLong: A game report from the Moncton Times-Transcript covers the Moncton Hawks' 4-1 AHL win over the Prince Edward Island Senators on February 27, 1994, with all four Hawks goals coming in the second period before 2,650 spectators at the Coliseum. Former Richmond Sockeye Dave Tomlinson is featured in a photo caption and noted in the game notes as having returned to the Hawks lineup after a 23-game stint with the NHL's Winnipeg Jets. The win improved Moncton's record to 31-26-3 (65 points), keeping them in second place in the Atlantic Division, eight points behind the St. John's Maple Leafs. Coach Rob Laird is quoted extensively on his team's slow start and recovery.

before.descriptionShort: Profile of former Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey, now with the Moncton Hawks, who hitchhiked from Los Angeles to British Columbia at age 15 to pursue hockey and escape a troubled home life.
before.descriptionLong: A front-page feature by Times-Transcript staff reporter Dwayne Tingley profiles Matt Hervey, a third-year defenceman with the Moncton Hawks and former Richmond Sockeyes player, who hitchhiked north from Los Angeles to Langley, British Columbia at age 15 to escape a difficult home situation and pursue hockey. Hervey reflects on how staying in L.A. might have led to a drug habit, and he now works with three teammates to raise awareness about the destructive effects of drugs and alcohol. The article continues on Page 8.
before.headline: Matt Hervey Profile: From Los Angeles to Hockey Career

before.descriptionShort: Profile of ex-Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey, crediting coach Orland Kurtenbach with turning his career around, as Hervey pursues a pro contract with the Winnipeg Jets' Moncton affiliate.
before.descriptionLong: The article profiles defenceman Matt Hervey, who credits Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach — a former NHL player — with improving his attitude and reviving his hockey career. Hervey recorded 24 points in 17 regular-season games with the Sockeyes and was named to the first all-star team as Richmond won the Centennial Cup national junior-A championship at Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Now in Moncton on a 25-game amateur tryout with the Winnipeg Jets' affiliate, Hervey is playing under coach Rick Bowness and is second on the team in penalty minutes (47) in just six games, with a contract potentially pending.

Former Richmond Sockeyes Dave Tomlinson skated for Boston University and drew a roughing penalty in the first period, while former Sockeyes netminder Chris Dickson started in goal for North Dakota, stopping 33 of 38 shots. A brief note describes Amonte breaking down the right wing on a 2-on-1 rush with linemate Tomlinson.

before.descriptionShort: UND hockey coach Gasparini announces six recruits for 1987–88, including Richmond Sockeyes forward Mike McCormick (6-3, 215), who had 26 goals and 24 assists in 51 regular-season games.
before.descriptionLong: UND hockey coach Gasparini announces six size-and-skill recruits for the 1987–88 season, among them Richmond Sockeyes forward Mike McCormick, a 6-3, 215-pound Washington native who scored 26 goals and 24 assists in 51 BCJHL regular-season games and added 15 goals in 13 playoff games as the Sockeyes competed in Centennial Cup post-season play. Gasparini praised McCormick's physical presence and versatility, noting he has the potential to play any forward position. Other recruits include players from Minnesota, the Alberta Junior Hockey League, and additional BCJHL clubs including the Langley Eagles and Penticton Knights.

Among them, Nonis and Wahlin emerged as top scorers, Studnicka plays center, and Czenczek developed into a solid defenseman. The article is relevant to the Sockeyes archive as Stan Czenczek was a member of the 1987 Richmond Sockeyes roster.

A goal was scored by Pasin deflecting Gordie Hynes' wrist shot while Hervey was in the penalty box.

after.descriptionShort: AHL game recap: Moncton defenseman Matt Hervey (ex-1987 Richmond Sockeyes) picked up multiple penalties as the Maine Mariners defeated Moncton 5-3.
after.descriptionLong: A recap of an AHL game in which the Maine Mariners rallied from three early goals allowed by goalie Bill Ranford to defeat Moncton 5-3. Former Richmond Sockeyes defenseman Matt Hervey of Moncton received a slashing minor, an interference minor (for interfering with John Carter in front of goalie Bob Essensa), an unsportsmanlike conduct minor, and a 10-minute misconduct for arguing with referee Mike North. While Hervey was serving the interference penalty, Maine's Pasin deflected a Gordie Hynes wrist shot from the point to score. The page includes a staff photo credited to John Ewing; no separate article byline is present in the OCR.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Chilliwack Eagles 10-1 in BCJHL play, with Dwayne Wills scoring three goals.

Dwayne Wills led the Coastal Division leaders with three goals, while Jason Talo added two. The Sockeyes held period leads of 2-1 and 7-1, outshoting their opponents 61-35. Additional scorers included Rick Rozecki, Barry Kress, Wayne Stripp, Steve Rutledge, and Garth Lamb.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Nanaimo Clippers 8-2 in BCJHL play, outshooting them 59-22, in a report that also covers Nanaimo's 4-3 OT win over Abbotsford Falcons.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes routed the Nanaimo Clippers 8-2 in BCJHL action, peppering the Nanaimo goaltending tandem with 59 shots while Frank Romeo stopped 22 in return. Matt Hervey, Dave Tomlinson, and Paul Rutherford each scored twice for Richmond, with singles from Mike McCormick and Russ Goglin; Parkes (36th) and Finner (23rd) replied for Nanaimo. A late third-period brawl, sparked when Sockeyes defenceman Jim Gunn ran Nanaimo goalie Flaherty, resulted in six ejections from each team. Coach Orland Kurtenbach noted the team was getting healthy: "We played well. We're starting to get players back from injuries and suspensions and we're starting to come together." The same article also reports Nanaimo's 4-3 overtime road win over Abbotsford Falcons, ending a 14-game winless streak.
before.headline: Clippers shade Falcons, bow to Sockeyes

before.descriptionShort: The Nanaimo Daily News previews the upcoming BCJHL playoff series between the Richmond Sockeyes and Nanaimo Clippers, profiling Richmond's top scorers and goaltender Frank Romeo ahead of the matchup.
before.descriptionLong: The Nanaimo Daily News previews the upcoming playoff series between the Richmond Sockeyes and Nanaimo Clippers. The article highlights Richmond's potent offence, led by league top goalscorer Rob Clarke (65 goals in 49 games), Dave Tomlinson (43-65-108), and Bryon Moller (27-64-91), plus goaltender Frank Romeo, who led the division with a 2.95 goals-against average. Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach stresses taking the series one game at a time, keeping the puck out of the net, and staying out of the penalty box, warning that Nanaimo's power play has hurt them in the past. Nanaimo coach Dutka discusses his own line-matching strategy, targeting Tomlinson with Corky Parkes and emphasizing playing their own game rather than reacting defensively.
before.headline: Sockeyes' Potent Lineup Takes On Clippers

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Nanaimo Clippers 7-4 in overtime in the opening game of the BCJHL Coastal Division semi-final playoff series on February 24, 1987, with Brian Kozak scoring his second goal of the game as the overtime winner at 3:28.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Nanaimo Clippers 7-4 in overtime on February 24, 1987, in the opening game of the BCJHL Coastal Division best-of-seven semi-final playoff series. The Clippers played competitively for the full 60 minutes, skating stride for stride with Richmond, but had trouble beating Sockeyes goalie Frank Romeo. Brian Kozak netted his second goal of the game at 3:28 of the 10-minute overtime session to clinch the win. Game two of the series was scheduled for that night in Richmond.

before.descriptionShort: Bill Hardy, a former Nanaimo Clipper now with the Richmond Sockeyes, is profiled ahead of game three of the BCJHL Coastal Division semi-final series at Civic Arena, with Richmond holding a 2-0 series lead.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes hold a 2-0 lead over the Nanaimo Clippers heading into game three of the BCJHL Coastal Division best-of-seven semi-final, scheduled for 8 p.m. at Civic Arena with over 1,300 fans expected. The article profiles Bill Hardy, who spent four years with Nanaimo — posting 39 goals and 72 assists in his best season — before joining Richmond in hopes of winning a championship and earning a college scholarship. With the Sockeyes, Hardy's role shifted to a grind-and-corner game behind top scorers Dave Tomlinson, Rob Clarke, and Bryan Moller, with linemates Brian Kozak and Jason Phillips; coach Orland Kurtenbach is quoted praising Hardy's contributions. A separate adjacent-column item also briefly identifies Don Rintala as a former Richmond Sockeyes captain, now playing fastball in New Zealand.
before.headline: Ex-Clipper Hardy Ready for Game Three

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes overcame a 3-2 third-period deficit — and the ejection of captain Trevor Dickie — to defeat the Nanaimo Clippers 7-3 in Game 3 of their BCJHL semi-final series, taking a 3-0 series lead before 1,415 fans at Civic Arena.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes rallied from a 3-2 deficit entering the third period to beat the Nanaimo Clippers 7-3 on February 27, 1987 in Game 3 of their best-of-seven BCJHL semi-final at Nanaimo's Civic Arena, giving Richmond a 3-0 series lead. A disputed last-second second-period goal by Corky Parkes resulted in the ejection of Sockeyes captain Trevor Dickie, but Richmond responded on the power play: Rob Clarke tied the game at 1:43 of the third, and Jason Phillips quickly put the Sockeyes ahead for good, with Dave Tomlinson, Mike McCormick, and Dean Rutledge (empty-netter) adding to the lead. Nanaimo goaltender Wade Flaherty stopped a remarkable 52 of 59 shots, while coach Orland Kurtenbach credited his team's composure after the controversial call; Game 4 was scheduled for the following night at Civic Arena.
before.headline: Sockeyes Withstand Nanaimo Pressure, Win Game 3

before.descriptionLong: The article previews the BC Junior Hockey League All-Star Game at Nicola Valley Memorial Arena in Merritt, with Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach heading the Coastal Division All-Stars. before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Humboldt Broncos 5-3 in Centennial Cup Western Canada final playoff game.
before.descriptionLong: Matt Hervey scored two goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 on Sunday in Centennial Cup playoff action, giving Richmond a 3-1 series lead. Goals from Brian Kozak, Dave Tomlinson, and Dave Rutherford supplemented Hervey's effort. Frank Romeo recorded 30 saves for the Sockeyes while Humboldt's Bruce Hoffort made 22 stops. Game six was scheduled for Tuesday night in Richmond.

The page also contains a sports column with additional notes on the Sockeyes' 21-6 post-season record and commentary on the Abbott Cup series location.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes routed visiting Abbotsford Falcons 11-1, with returning player Brian McFarlane scoring five goals and an assist, and Barry Kress adding three goals and two assists; Tony Bobbitt also scored for Richmond.
before.descriptionLong: Nanaimo Daily News BCJHL notes column reporting on the December 16 game in which the Richmond Sockeyes trounced visiting Abbotsford Falcons 11-1; Brian McFarlane — who had recently been traded to Kelowna Packers before returning to Richmond — scored five goals and an assist, while Barry Kress added three goals and two assists, and Dwayne Wills, Sasha Pogor, and Tony Bobbitt also scored. The column also highlights former Sockeyes in college hockey: goalie Jamie Stewart (ex-Langley Eagles) ranks second in CCHA goals-against average with Michigan State, and Steve Tuttle (ex-Richmond Sockeyes) is tied for sixth in WCHA scoring with 12 goals and 20 points for the Wisconsin Badgers.

after.descriptionLong: A sports notes column in the November 10, 1987 Nanaimo Daily News covers several BCJHL items involving the Richmond Sockeyes. Sockeyes player Rob Clarke dropped his gloves against Nanaimo Clipper Jack Piechutta in a Friday BCJHL game, explaining he "just got riled up" after being hit on the head. BCJHL standings show the Sockeyes leading with an 11-8-1 record, the Clippers second at 10-6-1, and the Nanaimo Whalers third at 7-7-1 — the Whalers having just earned an impressive home win over Richmond on Saturday in a game marred by a bench-clearing brawl with a minute remaining.

before.descriptionShort: Nanaimo Clippers defeat Juan de Fuca 5-2; Richmond Sockeyes lose to Delta Flyers 6-4.
before.descriptionLong: Game recap covering two BCJHL contests from October 14, 1987. Nanaimo Clippers downed Juan de Fuca 5-2 at Civic Arena behind goalie Mike Sutherland's third win since trade from Vernon, with player notes on Matt Woods and Rob Steele. Richmond Sockeyes fell to Delta Flyers 6-4 in Delta, with Brad Dickson scoring two goals for Richmond; Flyers' Holmes, Biagioni, and MacDonald combined for 12 points to maintain first place.

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes fall 6-4 to undefeated Delta Flyers; Rob Clarke and Mario Desjardins each score as Sockeyes drop to 6-5 in the Coastal Division.
after.descriptionLong: A Nanaimo Daily News recap of two October 13, 1987 BCJHL games includes a notable Richmond Sockeyes result: the Sockeyes lost 6-4 to the Delta Flyers in Delta, with Brad Dickson (two goals), Mario Desjardins, and Rob Clarke scoring for Richmond. Delta's Peter Holmes, Ben Biagioni, and Doug MacDonald combined for 12 points as the Flyers improved to a perfect 9-0 record to lead the Coastal Division; the Sockeyes sit second at 6-5. Sockeyes goalie Jason Palmer turned aside 37 shots. The article also recaps a Nanaimo Clippers 5-2 win over Juan de Fuca Whalers at Civic Arena, and notes the Clippers' next home game is against the Richmond Sockeyes on Friday.

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes and Juan De Fuca Whalers played to an 8-8 overtime tie in a penalty-filled BCJHL game Wednesday night in Richmond.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes and Juan De Fuca Whalers played to an 8-8 overtime tie in a penalty-filled BC Junior Hockey League game Wednesday night in Richmond. The Sockeyes appeared to have the game in hand until a string of penalties left them shorthanded for the final 14 minutes of the third period, during which the Whalers fired three power-play goals to equalize. Jason Talo scored twice for Richmond, while Mauro Padula, Mario Desjardins, Greg Hadden, Keith Fair, Rob Clarke, and Kirk Phare each added singles.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes and Juan De Fuca Whalers play to 8-8 overtime tie

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Delta Flyers 12-4 to take 2-0 lead in BCJHL playoff semifinal series.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes dominated visiting Delta Flyers with a 12-4 victory on Wednesday to claim a 2-0 lead in their best-of-seven BCJHL semifinal series. Barry Kress and Marty Comeau each scored three goals for Richmond, with additional goals from Jason Talo, Rob Clarke, Kirk Phare, Dan Walton, Garth Lamb, and Tony Bobbitt. The teams accumulated 450 minutes in penalties, including 99 minutes after a major fight in the second period. Richmond outshot Delta 55-24.
before.headline: Lakers oust Penticton / Sockeyes 12 Flyers 4

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes blasted visiting Delta Flyers 12-4 to take a 2-0 lead in their best-of-seven 1988 BCJHL playoff semifinal series.
after.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes dominated the visiting Delta Flyers 12-4 on Wednesday to claim a 2-0 lead in their best-of-seven BCJHL semifinal series. Barry Kress and Marty Comeau each scored three goals for Richmond, with additional tallies from Jason Talo, Rob Clarke, Kirk Phare, Dan Walton, Garth Lamb, and Tony Bobbitt. The teams accumulated 450 minutes in penalties, including 99 minutes assessed after a major fight in the second period, and Richmond outshot Delta 55-24. The page also carries a Canadian Press report on Vernon Lakers' 5-4 win over Penticton Knights to sweep their semifinal 4-0; Vernon will face the winner of the Richmond-Delta series.
after.headline: SOCKEYES 12 FLYERS 4

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Delta Flyers 6-5 in sudden-death overtime at Sungod Arena to clinch the BCJHL Coastal Division final series in four straight games, with Paul Rutherford scoring the winner at 4:37 of overtime.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes eliminated the Delta Flyers 6-5 in sudden-death overtime on Saturday at Sungod Arena, sweeping the BCJHL Coastal Division final series in four straight games and advancing to face the Kelowna Packers for the league championship. Paul Rutherford banged in a rebound at 4:37 of the extra session for the winner, after Delta's Momotani had tied it at 5-5 with 2:07 remaining in regulation. Delta goaltender Chris Dickson was spectacular in stopping 49 shots, drawing praise from Sockeyes head coach Orland Kurtenbach, who said the score would have been much higher without Dickson's performance. Flyers coach Rick Hopper acknowledged Richmond were the better team, while already looking ahead to rebuilding for next season.
before.headline: Sockeyes Eliminate Delta in Overtime

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Delta Flyers 6-5 in sudden-death overtime at Sungod Arena to clinch the BCJHL Coastal Division final series in four straight games, with Paul Rutherford scoring the winner at 4:37 of overtime.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes eliminated the Delta Flyers 6-5 in sudden-death overtime on Saturday at Sungod Arena, sweeping the BCJHL Coastal Division final series in four straight games and advancing to face the Kelowna Packers for the league championship. Flyers coach Rick Hopper acknowledged Richmond were the better team, while already looking ahead to rebuilding for next season.

Hervey had 11 points in 12 games and Lanz 14 points in 14 games; coach Ubriaco is quoted calling offense from the blue line "a strength of the team." before.descriptionShort: Toronto Maple Leafs defeat San Jose Sharks 5-1; prepare for Montreal matchup against Canadiens.
before.descriptionLong: The Toronto Maple Leafs defeated the San Jose Sharks 5-1 to climb back to .500 record. Glenn Anderson recorded five points (two goals, three assists) in the game. The Leafs now prepare to face the Montreal Canadiens at the Forum, with attention focused on returning coach Pat Burns and his relationship with Canadiens coach Jacques Demers.
before.headline: Maple Leafs back at .500 for tonight's Forum visit

after.descriptionShort: The Toronto Maple Leafs defeated the San Jose Sharks 5-1 to reach .500; ex-Sockeye Dave Tomlinson dressed for Toronto after replacing injured centre Peter Zezel.
after.descriptionLong: The Toronto Maple Leafs beat the San Jose Sharks 5-1 at Toronto, climbing to .500 with 41 points in 41 games, ahead of a visit to the Montreal Forum to face the Canadiens. Glenn Anderson recorded five points (two goals, three assists) and Grant Fuhr was sharp against a Sharks team outshot 43-24. Notably for this archive, ex-Richmond Sockeye Dave Tomlinson was recalled from Fredericton and dressed for the Leafs, replacing injured centre Peter Zezel. The game preview section also covers Pat Burns's return to Montreal as Toronto coach and a scouting report on both teams.
after.headline: Maple Leafs back at .500 for tonight's Forum visit

Mike Kelfer scored two goals, while Mike Sullivan contributed one goal and one assist. Dave Tomlinson and Sullivan also scored, with goalie Peter Fish recording his first career shutout with 29 saves.

after.descriptionShort: Former Richmond Sockeyes player Dave Tomlinson scored a third-period goal for Boston University in a 5-0 Hockey East shutout of the University of Lowell at Tully Forum.
after.descriptionLong: Boston University blanked the University of Lowell 5-0 in the Hockey East opener for both schools at Billerica's Tully Forum on October 31, 1987. Mike Kelfer scored twice and Mike Sullivan had a goal and an assist, while former Richmond Sockeyes player Dave Tomlinson added a third-period goal. Goalie Peter Fish recorded his first career shutout with 29 saves against 29 shots.
after.headline: Mike Kelfer Scores Twice as BU Shuts Out Lowell

before.descriptionShort: Box score and recap of Boston University defeating BC 6-3 in a Hockey East matchup.
before.descriptionLong: Boston University defeated BC 6-3 in a college hockey game played at Walter Brown Arena on February 28, 1988. The Terriers scored twice in the second period and three times in the third to secure the victory. Key scorers included Joe Sacco with two goals, and Mike Kelfer, Tom Dion, and Phil von Stefenelli each contributed goals. BC's David Littman made 38 saves while BU's Peter Fish stopped 29 shots.
before.headline: BU Beats BC 6-3 in Hockey East Game

after.descriptionShort: Boston University's Dave Tomlinson, a former Richmond Sockeye, recorded three assists as the Terriers defeated BC 6-3 in a Hockey East game at Walter Brown Arena on February 28, 1988.
after.descriptionLong: Former Richmond Sockeyes player Dave Tomlinson contributed three assists for Boston University in a 6-3 Hockey East victory over BC at Walter Brown Arena on February 28, 1988. Tomlinson assisted on goals by Mike Kelfer (2nd period), Tom Dion (3rd period), and Joe Sacco (3rd period). Joe Sacco led BU with two goals and an assist, while BC's David Littman made 38 saves in a losing effort and BU's Peter Fish stopped 29 shots. The box score and game recap appear on page 70 of The Boston Globe.
after.headline: BU Beats BC 6-3 in Hockey East Game

before.descriptionLong: Providence College defeated Boston University 6-4 at Walter Brown Arena on March 6, 1988, in a college hockey game. Providence's Andy Mattice scored twice, while Rick Bennett added a power-play goal with 5:39 remaining to secure the victory. BU's Mike Sullivan, Joe Sacco, and Tom Dion scored for the Terriers, who were outmatched despite mounting a comeback attempt. Providence's Gord Cruikshank achieved 96 career goals, becoming the school's all-time leading scorer.

Former Richmond Sockeyes player Dave Tomlinson, skating for BU, recorded an assist when Joe Sacco took his feed and scored to pull BU within one goal at 3-2. Andy Mattice scored twice for Providence and Rick Bennett added a power-play goal with 5:39 remaining to seal the win; Gord Cruikshank's 96th career goal made him Providence's all-time leading goal scorer.

Former Richmond Sockeyes player Dave Tomlinson contributed a goal and an assist for BU: he assisted on Ian Wood's opening tally and scored the go-ahead goal at 19:14 of the second period by redirecting a shot past Sioux goalie Scott Brower. Mike Kelfer led BU with two goals, while Grant Paranica scored both North Dakota goals. Peter Fish made 34 saves for BU against Brower's 26.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup semifinal in Humboldt, Saskatchewan.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes from British Columbia defeated Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a sudden-death semifinal game Thursday night at the 1987 Centennial Cup junior-A hockey tournament in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Richmond advanced to the championship final with a 2-1 round-robin record and would face Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the final Saturday night. Goal scorers included Dean Rutledge, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, and Jason Phillips.

before.descriptionShort: Dave Tomlinson scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes routed the Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 on April 17, 1987, winning the Centennial Cup Western best-of-seven semifinal 4-3.
before.descriptionLong: Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the visiting Richmond Sockeyes to a 9-1 thrashing of the Red Deer Rustlers on April 17, 1987, clinching the Centennial Cup Western best-of-seven semifinal 4-3. Richmond exploded for five goals in the second period, with additional goals from Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn, Mike McCormick, and Tony Bobbitt; the Sockeyes outshot the Rustlers 39-34. Red Deer's Pete DeGraaf spoiled goaltender Jamie Stewart's shutout bid at 13:17 of the third period. Richmond advances to face the Humboldt Broncos in the Western best-of-seven final.

before.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeat Richmond Sockeyes 2-1 in Centennial Cup Western Canadian playdowns, tying the best-of-seven series 2-2.
before.descriptionLong: Bill McDougall and Brad Bergen each scored as the Humboldt Broncos defeated the Richmond Sockeyes 2-1 on Saturday night in Centennial Cup Western Canadian playdowns, tying the best-of-seven series at 2-2. Dean Rutledge scored Richmond's lone goal in the third period. The game was played in Richmond, B.C., and the result was reported by Canadian Press.

before.descriptionShort: Profile of Richmond Sockeyes head coach Orland Kurtenbach, former Canucks captain, balancing his insurance business, a Surrey driving range, and junior hockey coaching while working 8 a.m. to midnight.
before.descriptionLong: Orland Kurtenbach, the first member of the NHL Canucks in 1970 who went on to captain and coach the team, is profiled juggling three ventures: a year-old general insurance business in New Westminster, a golf driving range under development in Surrey, and coaching the Richmond Sockeyes junior hockey team. Now 50 and just five pounds over his playing weight, Kurtenbach says owner Bruce Taylor called him in July to ask if he was interested in coaching, and despite initial hesitation over his business commitments, he agreed. Assistant coach Mike O'Brien is also mentioned. Kurtenbach describes his typical day as running from 8 a.m. to midnight.

before.descriptionShort: A Vancouver Sun article quotes hockey observer Phelps and Royals coach John Olver comparing the 1990 Surrey Royals unfavourably to the 1987 Centennial Cup champion Richmond Sockeyes, with both teams linked by owner Bruce Taylor.
before.descriptionLong: This Vancouver Sun clipping features commentary from a hockey observer named Phelps, who argues the 1987 Centennial Cup champion Richmond Sockeyes would "easily handle" the 1990 Surrey Royals, describing the Royals as a "half-million-dollar team" that "weren't as awesome as I expected" despite sweeping his team four straight. Royals coach John Olver disputes the spending figure, stating the operating budget is under $140,000, though league insiders suggest costs could reach $200,000–$300,000. The common thread between the two clubs is owner Bruce Taylor, described as a fierce competitor and Burnaby businessman, with Royals players finding employment at Taylor's roofing and sheet metal company rather than receiving direct pay.
before.headline: Royals compared to '87 Sockeyes championship team

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes will put their 15-game playoff winning streak on the line Tuesday against Alberta champion Red Deer Rustlers in the opening game of a best-of-seven Centennial Cup tier-two elimination series at Richmond Arena.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes will put their 15-game playoff winning streak on the line Tuesday at 7:30 p.m. when they host Alberta champion Red Deer Rustlers in Game 1 of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup tier-two junior hockey elimination series at Richmond Arena. The Sockeyes earned their spot with a 5-1 win over Quesnel Millionaires to take the Mowatt Cup in two straight — their fourth consecutive series sweep. Red Deer, who finished fourth in the Alberta Junior League, advanced by defeating the Calgary Spurs in seven games, Calgary Canucks in seven, and St. Albert Saints in six. Games 1 and 2 are in Richmond; the remainder of the series shifts to Red Deer.
before.headline: Sockeyes, Red Deer to clash

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes overwhelm Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in Game 1 of their Abbott Cup western semi-final series on April 7, 1987.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes dominated Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in the opening game of their Abbott Cup western semi-final series on April 7, 1987, taking a 1-0 lead in the best-of-seven. Red Deer coach Larry Billows called it the worst game his team had played, criticising poor defensive effort and goaltender Glen Furgason's performance. Sockeye goaltender Frank Romeo expressed surprise at the margin of victory, predicting a tighter Game 2 scheduled for April 8 at Richmond Arena. A photo shows defenceman Steve Jaques (#10) checking Red Deer's Pete deGraaf while Romeo watches the play from his crease.
before.headline: Red Deer Rustlers embarrassed in Centennial Cup semi-final loss to Richmond Sockeyes

before.descriptionShort: Bruce Taylor, owner of the Richmond Sockeyes, wins his fifth straight Coastal Division title as his New Westminster Royals defeat Powell River 7-5 in overtime, completing a comeback from a 3-1 series deficit.
before.descriptionLong: Burnaby businessman Bruce Taylor, former owner of the Richmond Sockeyes (1986–1988 Coastal Division champions, including the 1987 Centennial Cup), extended his streak to five straight BCJHL Coastal Division titles when his New Westminster Royals defeated the Powell River Paper Kings 7-5 in overtime, winning the best-of-seven final 4-3. Taylor's championship run spans three franchises: the Burnaby Bluehawks (1985), the Richmond Sockeyes (1986, 1987, 1988), and now the Royals (1989). New Westminster rallied from deficits of 2-0 and 3-1 in the series; Tony Bobbit scored the overtime winner at 3:07 of the extra period, and Blaine Moore added a shorthanded goal at 7:32 to clinch the win.
before.headline: Taylor Adds Fifth Title with Royals' Dramatic Comeback

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Kelowna Packers 7-5 to take a 3-0 series lead in the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship, with Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson, and Jason Phillips each scoring twice.
before.descriptionLong: A Canadian Press game report on page 24 of The Vancouver Sun, March 21, 1987, recaps Richmond Sockeyes' 7-5 playoff victory over Kelowna Packers in Kelowna, giving Richmond a 3-0 lead in the best-of-nine BCJHL championship series. Richmond jumped to a 4-0 first-period lead; Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson, and Jason Phillips each scored twice, with Mike McCormick adding a single. Coach Orland Kurtenbach credited intensity and physicality, while Kelowna coach Bill Hogaboam conceded his team was dominated. The page also includes a sports listings column noting the Richmond at Kelowna game 4 (8:00 p.m.) alongside NHL, Western League, soccer, and TV broadcast listings for the weekend.
before.headline: Sockeyes roll over Kelowna

before.descriptionShort: Article on Richmond Sockeyes preparing for Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt, featuring coach Orland Kurtenbach and owner Bruce Taylor.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes prepare for their national junior-A championship tournament opening Sunday in Humboldt. The team faces Atlantic champion Dartmouth in round-robin play, followed by Ontario's Pembroke and the host Humboldt Broncos. Coach Orland Kurtenbach and owner Bruce Taylor have rebuilt the roster with only nine players remaining from training camp and just one Richmond native on the squad.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes' National Championship Quest

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes prepare for the national junior-A Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt, with only nine players remaining from training camp and rookie Jason Talo as the sole Richmond native on the roster.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes open their national Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt on Sunday, facing Atlantic champion Dartmouth, then Ontario champion Pembroke, and host Humboldt Broncos in round-robin play. Owner Bruce Taylor praises head coach Orland Kurtenbach — a former Vancouver Canuck captain — crediting him with the team's success, including a notable moment of composure when fans in Red Deer threw eggs and fish at the Sockeyes' bench. The roster was heavily rebuilt, with only nine players remaining from training camp and just one Richmond native, rookie Jason Talo, on the squad. Captain Trevor Dickie, defenceman Mike Claringbull, and winger Dean Rutledge are among the players drawn from across the hockey world.
after.headline: Richmond Sockeyes' National Championship Quest

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes hold a 4-0 series lead over the Kelowna Packers after a 5-1 win Saturday, with a potential sweep on the line Tuesday at Richmond Arena.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes took a commanding 4-0 lead in their best-of-nine BCJHL championship series against the Kelowna Packers with a 5-1 road win Saturday, and can clinch a sweep Tuesday night at Richmond Arena. Coach Orland Kurtenbach praised the team's play and called on the players to limit mistakes heading into the home game. Jason Philips opened scoring with a shorthanded goal in the first period, Dave Tomlinson made it 2-0 before Kelowna's Brent Mowery cut the lead, and Richmond sealed the win with third-period goals from Philips, Bill Hardy, and Mike McCormick.
before.headline: Sockeyes one more win away

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes took a 4-0 series lead in the BCJHL championship playoffs with a 5-1 win over the Kelowna Packers on Saturday, one victory away from a sweep.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes moved to within one win of sweeping the best-of-nine BCJHL championship playoff series, taking a 4-0 lead with a 5-1 road victory over the Kelowna Packers on Saturday. Coach Orland Kurtenbach said the team was on a "natural high" and looked forward to clinching Tuesday night at Richmond Arena (7:30 p.m.). Jason Philips opened the scoring with a short-handed goal in the first period, Dave Tomlinson made it 2-0 early in the second before Kelowna's Brent Mowery cut the lead to 2-1, and Richmond then scored three unanswered third-period goals from Philips, Bill Hardy, and Mike McCormick.
before.headline: Sockeyes one more win away

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes win their first BCJHL championship in eight years, defeating Kelowna Packers 3-2 in Game 5 to complete a five-game sweep of the best-of-nine final.
before.descriptionLong: Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes captured their first BC Junior Hockey League championship in eight years on March 25, 1987, defeating the Kelowna Packers 3-2 at Richmond Arena to sweep the best-of-nine final series five games to none. The Sockeyes ran up 13 consecutive wins across three BCJHL playoff series, going through the entire playoffs without a loss. Captain Trevor Dickie hoisted the BC Junior League trophy alongside teammate Dean Rutledge (#20) and Rob Sumner. Kurtenbach, a former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach, expressed pride in winning in his first year of junior coaching, with the team's sights next set on the Centennial Cup national championship.
before.headline: The 'Eyes have it

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes edge Kelowna Packers 3-2 to sweep the BCJHL playoffs and claim their first B.C. Junior Hockey League championship in eight years.
before.descriptionLong: Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Kelowna Packers 3-2 on Tuesday, March 24, 1987, at Richmond Arena to claim their first BCJHL championship in eight years, completing 13 consecutive wins across three playoff series without a loss. Captain Trevor Dickie, a former New Westminster Bruin, expressed confidence in the team's national title chances, while key goals came from Jason Phillips (including a shorthanded marker) and Pat Mangold. The Sockeyes next face the Peace-Cariboo League champions in a best-of-nine series beginning April 3 in Richmond, with the Alberta Junior League champions to follow.
before.headline: Sockeyes sweep BCJHL playoffs

before.descriptionShort: Profile of Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Mike Claringbull pursuing aviation career over professional hockey.
before.descriptionLong: At the 1987 Centennial Cup in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Mike Claringbull discusses his passion for flying and aspiration to become a bush pilot rather than pursue a professional hockey career. The native of St. Lawrence, Newfoundland previously played for Medicine Hat Tigers and trained with the Montreal Canadiens organization. Claringbull is completing commercial pilot training while working toward his aviation goals.
before.headline: Sockeye vet has sights set on career in air

after.descriptionShort: Profile of Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Mike Claringbull, who moved from Pincher Creek to pursue aviation over pro hockey after two Montreal Canadiens training camps.
after.descriptionLong: At the 1987 Centennial Cup in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Mike Claringbull explains why he chose a flying career over professional hockey. A native of St. Lawrence, Newfoundland who moved to Pincher Creek, Alberta at age 10, Claringbull played for the Medicine Hat Tigers and attended two Montreal Canadiens training camps before declining a contract offer. With two weeks remaining in his commercial pilot training and a summer job lined up at Vancouver International Airport, Claringbull says his immediate goal is to become a bush pilot, with a longer-term aim of flying for a major airline.
after.headline: Sockeye vet has sights set on career in air

before.descriptionShort: Red Deer Rustlers defeat Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime to take a 3-2 series lead in the tier-two junior western semi-final, with Dixon Ward scoring the winner 59 seconds into overtime.
before.descriptionLong: The Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime Tuesday in Centennial Cup playoff hockey, Dixon Ward's goal 59 seconds into the 10-minute overtime period proving the winner. The victory gave Red Deer a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven tier-two junior western semi-final series, with Game 6 scheduled for the following night in Red Deer. Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach noted there was no repeat of the unruly fan behaviour that marred Sunday's match. Brian Kozak led Richmond with two goals, with Paul Rutherford, Mike Claringbull, and Jason Phillips adding singles; Phillips missed a late penalty shot before Geoff Gosselin sealed the result into an empty net.
before.headline: Fair Play Award

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Red Deer Rustlers 7-3 to even their western Centennial Cup semi-final series at 3-3, forcing a deciding game seven.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 7-3 in game six of their best-of-seven western Centennial Cup semi-final on April 15, 1987, in Red Deer, Alberta, evening the series at 3-3 and forcing a deciding game seven. The Sockeyes surged to a 5-1 lead after the first period before Red Deer narrowed the gap to 5-3, but a shorthanded goal by centre Brian Kozak early in the third period halted the Rustlers' comeback. Rob Clarke led Richmond with a hat trick, while Tony Bobbitt, Dave Tomlinson, and Mike McCormick each added singles. Coach Orland Kurtenbach credited Kozak's goal and improved officiating — which had begun calling instigating penalties rather than retaliation calls — for turning the series around.
before.headline: Sockeyes extend playoff to the limit

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes win Centennial Cup with 5-2 victory over Humboldt Broncos in Saskatchewan.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday night in Saskatchewan to clinch the Centennial Cup, Canada's tier two junior hockey championship. Goals from Dave Tomlinson, Jason Phillips, and Paul Rutherford led the Sockeyes to victory. Goaltender Frank Romeo earned tournament MVP honors, while Phillips was named player-of-the-game. The triumph capped a successful post-season in which Richmond won 27 of 34 playoff games and claimed five titles including the Mowat Cup, Abbott Cup, and Centennial Cup.
before.headline: Sockeyes add Centennial Cup to their silverware collection

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 in Saskatchewan to claim the Centennial Cup, with goaltender Frank Romeo named tournament MVP and coach Orland Kurtenbach's future with the club left uncertain.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 on Saturday in Humboldt, Saskatchewan to win the Centennial Cup, Canada's tier-two junior hockey championship. Goals by Dave Tomlinson and Jason Phillips in the first period set the tone, with Paul Rutherford adding a breakaway goal in the third; Phillips contributed a hat trick including the empty-netter. Goaltender Frank Romeo — player-of-the-game in all three of his tournament appearances — earned MVP honours, while Phillips took the most sportsmanlike player award. The victory capped a post-season in which Richmond won 27 of 34 playoff games and five titles (BCJHL, Mowat Cup, Alberta-BC, Abbott Cup, Centennial Cup); coach Orland Kurtenbach said he would decide over the summer whether to return to defend the title, with owner Bruce Taylor vowing not to pressure him.
after.headline: Sockeyes add Centennial Cup to their silverware collection
after.byline: By Elliott Pap, Sun Sports Reporter

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup in Saskatchewan.
before.descriptionLong: Coach Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday night in Humboldt, Saskatchewan to clinch the Centennial Cup, Canada's tier-two junior hockey championship. The victory capped a remarkable playoff run in which the Sockeyes won 27 of 34 games and captured five titles including the Centennial Cup. Jason Phillips scored a hat trick in the final game and earned tournament MVP honors, while goaltender Frank Romeo also received major individual awards.
before.headline: CANADIAN PRESS CHAMPAGNE SHOWER anoints Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup, Canada's tier-two junior hockey championship, in Humboldt, Saskatchewan.
after.descriptionLong: Coach Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday night in Humboldt, Saskatchewan to clinch the Centennial Cup, Canada's tier-two junior hockey championship. The victory capped a remarkable playoff run in which the Sockeyes won 27 of 34 games and captured five titles: the BC Junior League, Mowat Cup, Alberta-BC, Abbott Cup (western Canada), and the Centennial Cup. Jason Phillips capped a fantastic week with a hat trick in the final game and was selected tournament MVP, while goaltender Frank Romeo also took home major individual honours. Elliott Pap of the Vancouver Sun reported from Humboldt as the team prepared to bring the Cup home to Richmond.
after.headline: CHAMPAGNE SHOWER anoints Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes lose 5-3 to Humboldt Broncos in overtime in Game 1 of Centennial Cup Western final.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes fell 5-3 to the Humboldt Broncos in overtime on April 21, 1987, in Game 1 of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final. Curtis Chamberlain scored three goals including the overtime winner for Saskatchewan's champion Broncos. Richmond led 2-1 after two periods but the teams tied 3-3 in regulation, with Sockeyes scorers Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips.
before.headline: Sockeyes lose first in overtime

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes fall 5-3 to Humboldt Broncos, who scored twice in overtime, in Game 1 of the Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes lost 5-3 to the Humboldt Broncos on April 21, 1987, in Game 1 of the Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final after Humboldt scored twice in overtime. Curtis Chamberlain led the Saskatchewan champions with three goals including the overtime winner, while Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall added the others. Richmond had led 2-1 after two periods and the teams were tied 3-3 at the end of regulation, with Sockeyes goals from Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips.
after.headline: Sockeyes lose first in overtime

before.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeat Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 in overtime in opening game of Centennial Cup Western Canada final.
before.descriptionLong: The Humboldt Broncos scored twice in overtime to beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 on April 21, 1987, in the opening game of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final. Curtis Chamberlain scored three goals including the winner, while Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall added markers for Saskatchewan's champions. Richmond's goals came from Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips. The Sockeyes led 2-1 after two periods before the teams tied 3-3 at regulation.
before.headline: Sockeyes lose first in overtime

after.descriptionShort: The Humboldt Broncos beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3, scoring twice in overtime, to take game one of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final on April 20, 1987.
after.descriptionLong: The Humboldt Broncos scored twice in an overtime period to defeat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 on Monday, April 20, 1987, in the opening game of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Curtis Chamberlain scored three goals, including the overtime winner, for Saskatchewan's champion Broncos, with Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall adding the other markers. Richmond's goals came from Bryan Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips; the Sockeyes led 2-1 after two periods before the teams were tied 3-3 at the end of regulation.

issues: The draft description says 'scored twice in overtime' implying two separate overtime goals, but the OCR says 'scored twice in an overtime period' — this is a subtle but important distinction, and the score was 5-3 meaning only one overtime goal was needed after 3-3 regulation. The overtime period produced two goals total (one winner), which is plausible, but the phrasing in the draft is misleading.

The draft long description says 'Curtis Chamberlain scored three goals including the winner' which is accurate, but also repeats 'scored twice in overtime' which is ambiguous/misleading — the OCR says the Broncos scored twice in the overtime period to make it 5-3 from 3-3, which is consistent (two OT goals for Humboldt), so this is actually accurate on reflection.

The date in the short description says April 21, 1987, but the OCR says the game was played 'Monday night' — April 21, 1987 was a Tuesday, so the game was likely played Monday April 20, 1987, and the article was published April 21. The draft incorrectly states the game occurred on April 21.

The short description calls this the 'Centennial Cup Western Canada final' — the Centennial Cup IS the national championship; the Western Canada final would be the Abbott Cup. This appears to be what the OCR says ('Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final') so the draft is faithfully reproducing the OCR text, but it is worth noting.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-3 in Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final series.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, B.C. Junior League champions, defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 on April 22, 1987, to even their Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final series at one game each. The Sockeyes rebounded with an attitude adjustment to secure the win. Game three was scheduled for that evening in Humboldt before the series shifted venues.
before.headline: Sockeyes Even Series With Broncos

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-3 to even their Abbott Cup (Western Canada junior hockey final) series at one game each.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, B.C. Junior League champions, defeated the hometown Humboldt Broncos 5-3 on April 22, 1987, evening their best-of-seven Abbott Cup Western Canada junior hockey final series at one game each. According to the Sun News Services report, "an attitude adjustment" was all Richmond needed to get back on the winning track. Game three was scheduled for that same evening in Humboldt before the series was set to shift venues.
after.headline: Sockeyes Even Series With Broncos

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5–3 on Sunday at Richmond Arena to take the series lead in the Abbott Cup western final, with Dave Tomlinson scoring the winning goal.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes beat Saskatchewan's Humboldt Broncos 5–3 at Richmond Arena on Sunday to grab the series lead in the Abbott Cup western final, splitting the weekend's action. Dave Tomlinson scored the winning goal, and the team was already guaranteed a Centennial Cup berth regardless of the series result — since Humboldt is host of the national tournament — but coach Orland Kurtenbach's squad made clear they wanted to earn their spot through the front door. Goalie Frank Romeo (#29) and forward Matt Hervey (#22) were also visible in action during the game.

before.descriptionShort: Recap of Abbott Cup western final game six and preview of game seven, with Richmond Sockeyes facing Humboldt Broncos after a 4-3 overtime loss forced a deciding match on April 29, 1987.
before.descriptionLong: Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos were set to meet in game seven of the Abbott Cup junior-A western final on April 29, 1987, at Richmond Arena after Humboldt forced a decider by winning game six 4-3 in overtime on Mike Gaber's deflection at 3:31. Coach Orland Kurtenbach lamented two costly mistakes: Tony Bobbitt closing his hand on the puck, which led to Humboldt's tying power-play goal in the third period, and defenceman Steve Jaques receiving a five-minute match penalty in overtime after helmet-butting Bill McDougall. Paul Rutherford (twice) and Dave Tomlinson scored for Richmond in game six. Through six games the teams had played three overtime contests, with each club winning twice on the road, and Richmond leading the scoring aggregate just 21-20.

before.descriptionShort: Abbott Cup western final game seven between Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos scheduled for tonight at Richmond Arena.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos play game seven of their Abbott Cup western final series on April 29 at Richmond Arena. Humboldt forced the decider with a 4-3 overtime victory, with Mike Gaber scoring the winner. Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach cited defensive mistakes. Both teams are already assured spots in the upcoming Centennial Cup tournament.
before.headline: Sockeyes and Broncos Set for Game Seven

after.descriptionShort: Game seven of the Abbott Cup junior-A western championship between the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos is set for April 29 at Richmond Arena after Humboldt forced the decider with a 4-3 overtime win.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos meet in game seven of their Abbott Cup junior-A western championship series on April 29 at Richmond Arena (7:30 p.m.), with both clubs already guaranteed spots in the Centennial Cup. Humboldt forced the decider with a 4-3 overtime win, as Mike Gaber deflected Brad Bergen's point shot at 3:31 of extra time; Richmond was hurt by a hand-on-puck penalty to Tony Bobbitt and a five-minute match penalty to defenceman Steve Jaques for helmet-butting Bill McDougall. Paul Rutherford (two goals) and Dave Tomlinson scored for Richmond, and coach Orland Kurtenbach lamented the defensive mistakes that cost them the game. Through six games the teams are separated by just one goal (21-20 for Richmond), with three contests decided in overtime.

before.descriptionShort: Dave Tomlinson scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes routed the Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 to win the Centennial Cup Western best-of-seven semi-final 4-3, advancing to face the Humboldt Broncos.
before.descriptionLong: Dave Tomlinson scored three goals to pace the Richmond Sockeyes to a 9-1 victory over the Red Deer Rustlers on Thursday, clinching the Centennial Cup Western best-of-seven semi-final 4-3 in Red Deer. The Sockeyes advance to play the Humboldt Broncos in the next round. The page also carries a brief on the Brantford Mott's Clamatos defeating the St. John's Caps 4-2 in the Allan Cup Eastern Canada final, and a note on the Miramichi-Gagnon Packers offering to fund travel for the Stoney Plain Chiefs for a Canada Challenge Cup senior hockey series.

before.descriptionShort: Tony Bobbitt scored at 1:04 of overtime as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 4-3 to take a 2-1 lead in the Tier 2 western final (Abbott Cup) series.
before.descriptionLong: Tony Bobbitt's overtime goal at 1:04 gave the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in Game 3 of the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series, played Wednesday night in Humboldt. The victory put Richmond ahead 2-1 in the series, with Game 4 scheduled for Saturday in Richmond. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull, and Rob Clarke also scored for the Sockeyes, who were outshot 39-23; Bill McDougall (2) and Curtis Chamberlin replied for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. A brief note in the same article reports that the Pembroke Lumber Kings led their eastern final series 3-1 after an 8-2 win over Nickel Centre.

Game 6 was scheduled for Tuesday night in Richmond, B.C. This is a Canadian Press wire brief with no headline.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-4 to earn Centennial Cup tournament berth in best-of-seven Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey playoff series.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes advanced to the Centennial Cup tournament with a 5-4 victory over the Humboldt Broncos on April 29, 1987, clinching the best-of-seven Western Canada playoff series. Dave Tomlinson scored the winning goal with 2:37 remaining in regulation. The Sockeyes will compete in the Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt alongside Pembroke, Ontario and Dartmouth, Nova Scotia. The article also covers personnel changes with the Lloydminster Lancers hockey organization.
before.headline: Sockeyes gain Cup berth

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in game seven to clinch the Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey series and earn a berth in the 1987 Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes clinched the best-of-seven Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey series with a 5-4 victory over the Humboldt Broncos on April 29, 1987, winning four games to three. Dave Tomlinson scored the winner with 2:37 remaining in regulation, breaking a 4-4 tie; Matt Hervey, Dean Rutledge, Jason Phillips, and Bill Hardy also scored for Richmond. The Sockeyes will compete in the Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt alongside Pembroke, Ontario and Dartmouth, Nova Scotia. The Broncos were booed by Richmond fans after declining to participate in the traditional post-game handshakes at centre ice.
after.headline: Sockeyes gain Cup berth

before.descriptionShort: Centennial Cup round-robin results featuring Humboldt Broncos' victories and Richmond Sockeyes' 7-3 win over Dartmouth.
before.descriptionLong: During the 1987 Centennial Cup junior-A championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, the host Broncos defeated Dartmouth 7-2 and Pembroke 4-2 in round-robin play. The Richmond Sockeyes also competed, earning a 7-3 victory over Dartmouth with Jason Phillips scoring three goals. The tournament featured round-robin action with multiple teams competing for the Canadian junior-A championship title.
before.headline: Fast start for Broncos

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Dartmouth 7-3 in 1987 Centennial Cup round-robin play, with Jason Phillips scoring three goals; host Humboldt Broncos also won twice.
after.descriptionLong: In the 1987 Centennial Cup junior-A championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, the Richmond Sockeyes opened round-robin play with a 7-3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kings, led by Jason Phillips' hat trick; Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey also scored. The host Humboldt Broncos went 2-0 in round-robin action, defeating Dartmouth 7-2 and the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-2. Richmond stood at 1-0 after the results, with a game against Pembroke scheduled next.

before.descriptionShort: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 to win the Centennial Cup junior-A hockey championship, with Frank Romeo stopping 46 shots and earning MVP honours.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes won the Centennial Cup junior-A hockey championship by defeating the Humboldt Broncos 5-2, with Jason Phillips leading the offence with three goals. Goaltender Frank Romeo was the cornerstone of the victory, turning aside 46 of 48 Humboldt shots while the Sockeyes managed only 23, and was named both tournament and final-game MVP. Paul Rutherford scored the third-period winner and Dave Tomlinson also scored, as Richmond won before a standing-room crowd of 2,400 in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Broncos general manager and coach Bernie Lynch credited Richmond's aggressive play, calling them "deserving champions."
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup
