before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the host Humboldt Broncos 5-2 on Saturday night to win the Canadian junior-A hockey championship Centennial Cup. Goaltender Frank Romeo anchored the Sockeyes with 44 saves and was named the tournament's most valuable player and Richmond's player of the game. The Revelstoke native's strong performance secured the championship for the team.
before.headline: Champagne shampoo

after.descriptionShort: CP photo caption shows Richmond Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach receiving a champagne shower after the team's 5-2 Centennial Cup victory over the Humboldt Broncos.
after.descriptionLong: A CP wire photo captioned "Champagne shampoo" shows Sockeyes head coach Orland Kurtenbach being doused by team members following Richmond's 5-2 win over the host Humboldt Broncos for the Canadian junior-A Centennial Cup championship on Saturday night. Goaltender Frank Romeo anchored the victory with 44 saves — compared to 18 for Humboldt's Bruce Hoffort — and was named both Richmond's player of the game and the tournament's most valuable player. Romeo, a Revelstoke native, dedicated the performance to his parents who were in the stands.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes win 1987 Centennial Cup championship with 5-2 victory over Humboldt Broncos.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes captured the Centennial Cup Canadian junior-A championship on May 10, 1987, defeating the Humboldt Broncos 5-2. Goaltender Frank Romeo earned most valuable player honours. The victory marks BC's second consecutive Canadian junior-A title. Most of Richmond's experienced core, including defenceman Matt Hervey, will age out as 20-year-olds and depart the team.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup

after.descriptionShort: After winning the 1987 Centennial Cup, the Richmond Sockeyes face a near-complete roster turnover, with only teenagers Rob Sumner and Jason Talo expected to return.
after.descriptionLong: Frank Luba reports that the Richmond Sockeyes' Centennial Cup triumph — a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos on May 9, 1987 — is likely the last game together for most of the squad: nine 20-year-olds, including MVP goaltender Frank Romeo and defenceman Matt Hervey, are no longer eligible, leaving only 16-year-olds Rob Sumner and Jason Talo set to return. Coach Orland Kurtenbach and president Horst Willkomm must also decide their own futures. The article also notes that Humboldt Broncos head coach Bernie Lynch drew fan outrage for his team's refusal to shake hands with the Sockeyes after Richmond clinched the Western Canadian championship.
after.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup

The draft omits key detail that only Rob Sumner and Jason Talo (both 16-year-olds) are expected to return to the Sockeyes.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-4 to win Abbott Cup 4-3 series, advancing to Centennial Cup.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in Game 7 of the Abbott Cup Western Canadian junior-A championship series, winning 4-3. As B.C. champions, the Sockeyes have now eliminated Nanaimo, Delta, Kelowna, and Red Deer. The Broncos, as Centennial Cup hosts in Humboldt, will face Richmond again starting Saturday in the Canadian championship tournament.
before.headline: Sockeyes Add Humboldt Broncos to Hit List

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat the Humboldt Broncos 5-4 to win the Abbott Cup Western Canadian junior-A championship series 4-3, advancing to the Centennial Cup.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in Game 7 to win the Abbott Cup Western Canadian junior-A championship series 4-3, adding the Saskatchewan champions to their list of vanquished opponents — Nanaimo, Delta, Kelowna, and Red Deer. The Broncos, as Centennial Cup hosts in Humboldt, will face Richmond again starting Saturday in the national championship tournament. The Broncos were booed by a finally-noisy Richmond Arena crowd for failing to participate in the traditional post-game handshakes at centre ice.
after.headline: Add Humboldt Broncos to the Richmond Sockeyes' Hit List

before.descriptionShort: Frank Luba previews the Centennial Cup final between the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos, noting both clubs have been called evenly matched — and now find themselves mutual underdogs heading into the championship.
before.descriptionLong: In a May 10, 1987 Sports Reporter column from Humboldt, SK, Frank Luba sets up the Centennial Cup final by observing that the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos have long been considered evenly matched, leaving both teams cast as underdogs against each other. The page also carries the Centennial Cup all-star selections announced before semifinal play: Humboldt's Bill McDougall (4G, 3A, tied for round-robin scoring lead), Rob Rice (defence), and Duncan Ryhorchuk (right wing); Richmond's Jason Phillips (3G, 3A, second in scoring) and Matt Hervey (defence); and Pembroke goaltender Grant Robb.

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes advanced to the Centennial Cup western final against host Humboldt Broncos after defeating Alberta champion Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 on April 17, taking the western semifinal series 4-3; the Sockeyes are guaranteed a Centennial Cup berth regardless of the western final result.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes clinched a berth in the Centennial Cup national championship tournament and advanced to the western final against host Humboldt Broncos by defeating the Alberta champion Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 on April 17, taking the best-of-seven western semifinal 4-3. Dave Tomlinson led the scoring with three goals, while Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn, Mike McCormick, and Tony Bobbitt also scored; Red Deer's Pete DeGraaf spoiled Jamie Stewart's shutout bid. Because Humboldt is also the Centennial Cup host, the Sockeyes are guaranteed a national tournament spot even if they lose the western final, which opens April 20-21 in Humboldt with the balance of the series shifting to Richmond Arena if needed.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes bound for Centennial Cup either way

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Kelowna Packers 3-2 to win the BCJHL championship series in five games, extending their playoff winning streak to 13.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes edged the Kelowna Packers 3-2 on March 24, 1987 at Richmond Arena, winning the best-of-nine BCJHL championship series in five games before more than 1,100 fans. Jason Philips scored the winner at 9:33 of the third period, flipping a rebound past Kelowna goaltender Dusty Imoo after Pat Mangold had tied the game 2-2 on a power play; goaltender Frank Romeo was credited for a strong performance despite battling sinusitis. The win stretched Richmond's playoff winning streak to 13 games and set up a Mowat Cup provincial final, tentatively scheduled for April 3-5 in Richmond, against the PCJHL champion. Veteran defenceman Matt Hervey, who suffered a shoulder separation in Kelowna on Friday, watched the third period from the parking lot while rehabilitating.
before.headline: Packers 2 Sockeyes 3

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Kelowna Packers 3-2 to win the BCJHL championship series in five games, extending their playoff winning streak to 13.

before.descriptionShort: Vernon Lakers defeat defending champion Richmond Sockeyes 6-2 in BCJHL league final Game 3.
before.descriptionLong: The defending B.C. Junior Hockey League champion Richmond Sockeyes were defeated 6-2 by the Interior Division champion Vernon Lakers at Richmond Arena on March 22, 1988. Vernon leads the best-of-seven league final 3-0 and could clinch the series in Game 4 that evening in Richmond. The Lakers displayed swift skating and passing prowess in their dominant performance.

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes were defeated 6-2 by the Vernon Lakers at Richmond Arena, putting the defending BCJHL champions down 3-0 in the best-of-seven league final.
after.descriptionLong: The defending B.C. Junior Hockey League champion Richmond Sockeyes were defeated 6-2 at Richmond Arena by the swift-skating Vernon Lakers on the night of March 22, 1988. Vernon, the Interior Division champion, leads the best-of-seven league final 3-0 and could eliminate the Coastal Division champions in Game 4 that same evening. The article includes a staff photo by Rick Loughran.

Prince George also advanced in Peace-Cariboo Junior Hockey League playoffs, with the series tied 1-1. The winner meets Vernon in the Mowat Cup final.

before.descriptionShort: Mike McCormick scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Juan de Fuca Whalers 6-2 in a BCJHL game at Juan de Fuca Recreation Centre.

before.descriptionShort: BCJHL season preview covers Interior and Coastal divisions, noting Richmond Sockeyes' expectations for 1987-88 season.
before.descriptionLong: This Times Colonist preview article surveys the 1987-88 British Columbia Junior Hockey League season across Interior and Coastal divisions. The Richmond Sockeyes, who finished first last season with a 38-14 record, are expected to take a back seat to Interior rivals Kelowna and Penticton. Coach Ernie Gare expresses confidence in the team's defensive prospects. Vernon Lakers are touted as Interior favorites; Delta Flyers favored in Coastal Division.
before.headline: How the season shapes up

after.descriptionShort: A Canadian Press preview of the 1987-88 BCJHL season surveys all divisions, noting the Richmond Sockeyes' first-place 38-14 finish in 1986-87 and their expected challenge from Delta Flyers in the Coastal Division.
after.descriptionLong: This Canadian Press preview of the 1987-88 British Columbia Junior Hockey League season covers both the Interior and Coastal divisions ahead of opening night. The Richmond Sockeyes, who finished first in the Coastal Division with a 38-14 record the prior season after bolstering their roster with WHL veterans, are expected to face a stiff challenge from the heavily-recruited Delta Flyers. In the Interior Division, the Vernon Lakers — not Kelowna or Penticton — are touted as the team to beat, with coach Ernie Gare (of Vernon, not Richmond) expressing confidence in the Lakers' defensive depth and recruiting. New franchise Salmon Arm Tigers and the transfer of Langley Eagles to Chilliwack are also noted as league changes.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes win the Centennial Cup championship with a 5-2 victory over Humboldt Broncos.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 on Saturday night in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, to win the Canadian Tier II Junior A hockey championship. Jason Phillips scored three goals, while Paul Rutherford and Dave Tomlinson added one each. Goaltender Frank Romeo made 46 saves and was named tournament MVP, holding the Sockeyes' opponents to just 23 shots.

Jason Phillips scored three goals, with Paul Rutherford adding the game-winner in the third period and Dave Tomlinson also scoring. Goaltender Frank Romeo was the story, stopping 46 shots while his teammates managed only 23 on the Broncos' net; Romeo was named tournament MVP.

before.descriptionShort: A Canadian Press wire story on Orland Kurtenbach, former Vancouver Canucks captain and coach, returning to hockey as head coach of the BCJHL's Richmond Sockeyes in 1986.
before.descriptionLong: A Canadian Press report from Richmond, B.C. profiles Orland Kurtenbach's return to hockey coaching at the Tier Two junior level with the Richmond Sockeyes in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Kurtenbach, now 50 and only five pounds over his playing weight, was the first member of the Vancouver Canucks franchise in 1970, later captaining and then coaching the NHL club. He balances a demanding schedule — averaging 8 a.m. to midnight — between his regular job and coaching, and is quoted saying coaching is "nothing but a hobby" with no desire to return to full-time professional coaching. The OCR is truncated mid-sentence; the full article text may contain additional detail.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Pembroke 4-1 in Centennial Cup round robin; face Humboldt Broncos Wednesday.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 on Monday in Centennial Cup round-robin play at the Humboldt Uniplex, improving to 2-0 in the tournament. Dave Tomlinson scored two third-period goals to secure the victory. Richmond will face Humboldt Broncos on Wednesday in the final round-robin game, with the winner advancing directly to Saturday's championship game. Coach Orland Kurtenbach's team killed multiple penalties during the contest.

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes beat Pembroke 4-3 to go 2-0 in Centennial Cup round robin, setting up a winner-take-all showdown with Humboldt Broncos on Wednesday.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-3 on Monday to move to 2-0 in the Centennial Cup round robin at the Humboldt Uniplex, tying Humboldt Broncos atop the standings. Dave Tomlinson scored two third-period goals to seal the win, while coach Orland Kurtenbach credited the team for killing numerous penalties. The win sets up a decisive Wednesday clash between Richmond and Humboldt — teams with a heated prior seven-game series rivalry — with the winner earning an automatic berth in Saturday's championship final. Defenceman Steve Jaques summed up the mood: "We know who we have to beat to win the Centennial Cup."

Sockeyes coach Orland Kurtenbach criticised Broncos player Bernie Lynch's refusal to shake hands after their earlier series, while Richmond's Steve Jaques — who started the season with Humboldt — offered a sympathetic take, saying he wouldn't have wanted to shake hands with opponents either. On the recruitment front, several Sockeyes are bound for American college programs: Dave Tomlinson to Boston University, Mike McCormick to North Dakota, Paul Rutherford to Ohio State, and Bryon Moller to Alabama, with Pembroke and Humboldt players also drawing significant NCAA interest at the tournament.

before.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeat Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play.
before.descriptionLong: Humboldt Broncos dominated Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play on May 7, 1987. The lopsided victory saw the Broncos outscore and outplay Richmond in all aspects. Coach Rich Kurtenbach acknowledged the loss, stating the team lacked intensity. Richmond finished the round robin 2-1 and will face Pembroke in the semi-final, with the Broncos (3-0) advancing directly to the championship.
before.headline: Humboldt dominates Richmond in Centennial Cup round robin

after.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeat Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play, with goals from Stan Czenczek and Steve Jaques involved in key plays.
after.descriptionLong: The Humboldt Broncos dominated the Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play on May 7, 1987, in what turned into one of the most lopsided results in eight games between the two Western finalists. Stan Czenczek tied the game 1-1 in the first period with a hard slapshot, but Humboldt's Garnet Kazuik scored a shorthanded breakaway goal in the second period after stealing the puck from Steve Jaques, opening the floodgates. Coach Orland Kurtenbach lamented "We got outworked, outplayed, out-everythinged — the 'oomph' just wasn't there." Richmond finished the round robin 2-1 and advanced to a semi-final against Pembroke, with the Broncos (3-0) going directly to the championship game.

before.descriptionLong: Richmond defeated Humboldt 5-2 in the Centennial Cup championship final on May 11, 1987. Tomlinson, Phillips (twice), and Rutherford scored for Richmond, with Humboldt goals from Chamberlin and Luke. Richmond goaltender Romeo and Humboldt's Hoffort managed shots of 23-46 respectively. The box score includes complete penalty details and power-play statistics.
before.headline: Richmond 5 Humboldt 2 Championship Final Saturday

after.descriptionShort: Box score of Richmond Sockeyes' 5-2 victory over Humboldt Broncos in the Centennial Cup championship final, played Saturday May 9, 1987.
after.descriptionLong: Richmond defeated Humboldt 5-2 in the Centennial Cup championship final on Saturday, May 9, 1987 (published May 11). Sockeyes goals came from Tomlinson, Phillips (3), and Rutherford; Humboldt replied with goals from Chamberlin (power play) and Luke. Richmond goaltender Romeo faced 46 shots while Humboldt's Hoffort faced 23; Richmond went 0-for-3 on the power play while Humboldt converted 1 of 5. The box score includes complete period-by-period scoring, penalty details, and shot totals.
after.headline: Richmond 5 Humboldt 2 Championship Final Saturday

issues: Date error: the draft states 'May 11, 1987' as the game date, but the Centennial Cup final was played May 9, 1987 — May 11 is the newspaper publication date, not the game date. The box score headline says 'Championship Final Saturday' (Saturday = May 9).

Shot totals are reversed: the draft says 'Romeo...23' and 'Hoffort...46', but these are shots ON goal, meaning Romeo faced 46 shots (Humboldt outshot Richmond 46-23), not 23. The draft's phrasing implies Romeo had 23 shots, which is misleading.

Goal scorers slightly mis-described: Phillips scored twice (goals 2 and 5) in the first and second periods, plus a third (goal 7) in the third period — Phillips actually scored three goals, not two. The draft says 'Phillips (twice)' but the OCR shows three Richmond goals by Phillips (lines 2, 5, 7).
Headline is slightly paraphrased; actual headline from OCR reads 'Richmond 5 Humboldt 2 Championship Final Saturday'.

before.descriptionShort: Box score from a 1986 college hockey game between Saskatchewan and UBC, notable for listing Stan Czenczek (later a 1987 Richmond Sockeyes player) as a UBC player who drew two penalties.
before.descriptionLong: Box score and game summary from a college hockey matchup between the University of Saskatchewan Huskies and UBC, published in the Star-Phoenix on November 29, 1986. Saskatchewan won the game, outshooting UBC 43–31, with Sakundiak recording two goals and converting 3-of-12 power plays while UBC went 0-for-8. The scan is archive-relevant because Stan Czenczek — later a player on the 1987 Centennial Cup–winning Richmond Sockeyes — appears in the UBC penalty summary, drawing hooks and holding minors.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 1-1 in Abbott Cup series, with Jason Phillips scoring three goals in Game Two.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes evened their best-of-seven Abbott Cup series against the Humboldt Broncos at one win apiece on April 22, 1987. Richmond won Game Two at the Humboldt Uniplex, with Jason Phillips scoring three goals and adding an assist. Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch criticized his team's lack of intensity, noting the loss ended their 17-game winning streak. Game Three was scheduled for that evening at 7:30 in Humboldt.
before.headline: Sockeyes Even Abbott Cup Series with Humboldt

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes even their Abbott Cup best-of-seven series against the Humboldt Broncos at one win apiece, with Jason Phillips scoring three goals and adding an assist in Game Two.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos in Game Two of their Abbott Cup series on April 22, 1987, tying the best-of-seven at one win apiece. The loss snapped Humboldt's 17-game winning streak, prompting coach Bernie Lynch to criticize his team's lack of hunger and intensity. Jason Phillips led Richmond offensively with three goals and an assist, crediting linemates Brian Kozak and Rob Clarke for setting him up throughout the game. Game Three was scheduled for that evening at 7:30 at the Humboldt Uniplex.
after.headline: Sockeyes Even Abbott Cup Series with Humboldt

The OCR confirms the article is about the Abbott Cup series, NOT the Centennial Cup final. The Abbott Cup is the Western Canada final, not the national championship.
The short description's phrasing is factually garbled: 'defeat Humboldt Broncos 1-1' is nonsensical — a team cannot defeat another team 1-1.

before.descriptionShort: Box score of Richmond Sockeyes defeating Humboldt 5-3 in Game 2 of the Centennial Cup Western final, tying the best-of-seven series 1-1.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 in Game 2 of the Centennial Cup Western final on April 22, 1987, tying the best-of-seven series 1-1. Jason Phillips led Richmond with a hat trick (goals 3, 5, and 8), while Czenczek and Kozak each added a goal; two of Richmond's goals came on the power play in the second period. The detailed box score lists all penalties, goaltenders (Humboldt: Backlund/Lloyd; Richmond: Romeo), shots on goal (Humboldt 45, Richmond 31), and power-play statistics.

Richmond won 4–3 in overtime on a goal by Bobbitt at 1:04 of OT, with assists from McCormick and Czenczek. The game featured seven goals total and extensive penalty minutes across all three periods plus overtime.

after.descriptionShort: Game summary sheet for a 1987 playoff game in which Richmond Sockeyes defeated Humboldt 4–3 in overtime on Bobbitt's goal at 1:04 OT, with Clarke's tying goal at 19:27 of the third period setting up the win.
after.descriptionLong: Period-by-period goal and penalty summary for a 1987 playoff game between the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt, with Richmond winning 4–3 in overtime. Sockeyes goals came from Tomlinson (shorthanded, second period), Claringbull (third period), Clarke (tying goal at 19:27 of the third, assisted by Kozak and Tomlinson), and Bobbitt's overtime winner at 1:04 assisted by McCormick and Czenczek. The game featured heavy penalty totals across all periods, including a major to Gunn (highsticking) and a late bench minor to Richmond for too many men in overtime.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 to take a 3-2 series lead in their Abbott Cup best-of-seven, with Matt Hervey scoring twice for Richmond.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, BC junior-A champions, defeated the Humboldt Broncos 5-3 on Sunday to lead their Abbott Cup best-of-seven series 3-2, with both clubs set to participate in the Centennial Cup tournament beginning Saturday in Humboldt. Humboldt, Saskatchewan Amateur Junior Hockey League champions, led 2-0 after the first period before Richmond tied it 2-2 in the second frame. Matt Hervey scored twice for Richmond. Broncos coach Bernie Lynch criticized his team's play, saying "We haven't come close to reaching our potential in this series."
before.headline: Humboldt Broncos fall to Richmond Sockeyes in Abbott Cup series

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in Game 7 of Abbott Cup series to advance to Centennial Cup tournament.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes won the Abbott Cup best-of-seven series four games to three over the visiting Humboldt Broncos, with Dave Tomlinson scoring the series-clinching goal with 2:37 remaining in Game 7 on Wednesday night. The tightly contested series saw each team win two one-goal games, with Richmond outscoring Humboldt 26-24 overall. Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch praised his team's effort despite the loss, noting the Centennial Cup tournament officially opens in Humboldt on Friday.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Abbott Cup, Edge Humboldt 5-4

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat visiting Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in Game 7 to win the Abbott Cup Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey series four games to three.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes won the Abbott Cup best-of-seven Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey series four games to three over the Humboldt Broncos, with Dave Tomlinson scoring the series-clinching goal with 2:37 remaining in regulation time Wednesday night to snap a 4-4 tie. Richmond outscored Humboldt 26-24 across the series, with each team winning two one-goal games. Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch praised his team's effort but noted that a Centennial Cup win over Richmond would render the Abbott Cup result moot. The page also includes a companion article on the Broncos' strong financial preparations for hosting the Centennial Cup tournament, which officially opens in Humboldt on Friday.
after.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Abbott Cup, Edge Humboldt 5-4

before.descriptionShort: B.C. Junior Hockey League 1987 playoff final statistics, showing Richmond Sockeyes going undefeated through quarterfinals, semifinals, and the league final against Kelowna Packers, with top scorers including Dean Rutledge, Dave Tomlinson, Jason Phillips, and Brian Kozak of Richmond.
before.descriptionLong: The Abbotsford News published the B.C. Junior Hockey League 1987 final playoff statistics on April 11, 1987. The Richmond Sockeyes went undefeated through all three rounds: 4–0 in the quarterfinals (over Nanaimo Clippers), 4–0 in the semifinals (over Delta Flyers), and 5–0 in the league final over the Kelowna Packers. Top Sockeyes scorers were Jason Phillips (20 pts in 13 games), Dean Rutledge and Dave Tomlinson (21 pts each), and Brian Kozak (19 pts); Kerry Russell of Kelowna led all scorers with 26 points.
before.headline: B.C. Junior Hockey League final statistics: Playoffs

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes lose opening game 5-3 to Humboldt Broncos in best-of-seven Western Canada Centennial Cup playoff.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, B.C. Junior Hockey League champions, lost 5-3 to the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in overtime during game one of the best-of-seven Western Canada Centennial Cup championship series. Despite the loss, both teams are guaranteed spots in the national four-team final. Sockeyes scorers included Bryon Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips.
before.headline: Sockeyes assured of Cup play

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes lost 5-3 in overtime to the Humboldt Broncos in game one of the best-of-seven Western Canada Centennial Cup championship, but both teams are guaranteed spots in the national four-team final.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes, B.C. Junior Hockey League champions, dropped game one of the best-of-seven Western Canada Centennial Cup championship to the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos 5-3, with Humboldt scoring twice in a 20-minute overtime period. Despite the loss, both the Sockeyes and Broncos are assured of participating in the national four-team Centennial Cup final, as Humboldt is the host city and both western finalists are guaranteed berths. Sockeyes scorers in game one were Bryon Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips. The article notes the Sockeyes had previously defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 in the seventh and deciding game of the B.C.-Alberta playoff.
after.headline: Sockeyes assured of Cup play

before.descriptionShort: Preview of Richmond Sockeyes 1987-88 season following their Centennial Cup championship, highlighting returning players and recruitment efforts under coach Orland Kurtenbach.
before.descriptionLong: The BC Junior Hockey League begins its 1987-88 season with the Richmond Sockeyes looking to repeat their success after winning the Centennial Cup in spring 1987. Though only four players return from the championship roster, coach Orland Kurtenbach's strong recruitment program is expected to maintain competitiveness. Returning players include goalie Glenn Pencer (fifth season), right winger Rob Clarke (65 goals, 35 assists last year), centre Jason Tallo, and right winger Tony Bobbit.
before.headline: <UNKNOWN>

after.descriptionShort: Preview of the Richmond Sockeyes' 1987-88 BCJHL season, highlighting their Centennial Cup title, returning players including goalie Glenn Pencer and scorer Rob Clarke, and coach Orland Kurtenbach's recruitment approach.
after.descriptionLong: A Chilliwack Progress column reprinted in The Abbotsford News previews the 1987-88 BCJHL season with a focus on the Richmond Sockeyes, who won the Centennial Cup in spring 1987. Only four players return from the championship roster — none on defence — including five-year veteran goalie Glenn Pencer (4.20 GAA, shared duties with Frank Romeo), leading scorer Rob Clarke (65 goals, 35 assists, third in Coast Division), centre Jason Tallo, and right winger Tony Bobbit. Despite the thin returning core, coach Orland Kurtenbach's strong recruiting record is cited as the reason the Sockeyes are expected to remain competitive in the Coastal Division.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes' Tony Bobbitt scored for the Coastal all-stars in a BCJHL all-star game against an Interior squad in Merritt, with Kelowna Packers goaltender Lucien Carignan named Interior MVP after shutting out Coastal in the final 30 minutes.
before.descriptionLong: A BCJHL all-star game pitting Coastal and Interior teams saw Richmond Sockeyes forward Tony Bobbitt score for the Coastal squad alongside goals from Darren Biggs and Mike Knapton of Nanaimo Clippers and Aaron Nosky of Delta Flyers. Interior scorers included Pat Mangold and Craig Bedard of Kelowna Packers and Darryl Kirkpatrick and Steve Manuel of host Merritt Warriors. The Coastal all-stars held leads of 4-0 and 5-3 at period breaks but were outshot 42-32, and Kelowna Packers goaltender Lucien Carignan was named Interior MVP after blanking the Coastal side in the game's final 30 minutes. The OCR page also contains an unrelated notice that the Richmond Sockeyes were hosting Abbotsford with a 7:30 p.m. game time at Richmond Arena.
before.headline: All-Star Hockey Game Results

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes' Tony Bobbitt scored for the Coastal all-stars in a BCJHL all-star game against an Interior squad in Merritt, with Kelowna Packers goaltender Lucien Carignan named Interior MVP after shutting out Coastal in the final 30 minutes.
after.descriptionLong: A BCJHL all-star game pitting Coastal and Interior teams saw Richmond Sockeyes forward Tony Bobbitt score for the Coastal squad alongside goals from Darren Biggs and Mike Knapton of Nanaimo Clippers and Aaron Nosky of Delta Flyers. Interior scorers included Pat Mangold and Craig Bedard of Kelowna Packers and Darryl Kirkpatrick and Steve Manuel of host Merritt Warriors. The Coastal all-stars held leads of 4-0 and 5-3 at period breaks but were outshot 42-32, and Kelowna Packers goaltender Lucien Carignan was named Interior MVP after blanking the Coastal side in the game's final 30 minutes. The OCR page also contains an unrelated notice that the Richmond Sockeyes were hosting Abbotsford with a 7:30 p.m. game time at Richmond Arena.
after.headline: All-Star Hockey Game Results

before.descriptionShort: The Langley Eagles trail the Richmond Sockeyes by three points in the BCJHL Coastal Division standings despite having traded top scorer Bryan Moller to Richmond in exchange for defenceman Philip Von Stefenelli.
before.descriptionLong: The Langley Eagles are three points behind the Richmond Sockeyes in the BCJHL Coastal Division playoff race, having won three of their last four games without top scorer Bryan Moller after trading him to Richmond. Eagles coach and general manager Muzz MacPherson defended the deal, conceding "we gave them a lot of points" by trading Moller but saying the team needed a defenceman who could move the puck well. In return, Langley received defenceman Philip Von Stefenelli from Richmond.
before.headline: Langley Eagles Hot on Richmond Sockeyes Trail Despite Trading Top Marksman

before.descriptionShort: A Langley Advance columnist second-guesses his earlier praise of the Langley Eagles' trade of Bryan Moller to the Richmond Sockeyes, noting Moller scored in Richmond's 5-3 Game 1 loss to the Humboldt Broncos in what the article calls the Centennial Cup final.
before.descriptionLong: A Langley Advance sports columnist revisits his earlier endorsement of Langley Eagles coach Muzz MacPherson's decision to trade scoring ace Bryan Moller to the Richmond Sockeyes, admitting he may have been wrong given Moller's strong contributions to the Sockeyes' deep playoff run. The column notes Moller scored a goal in Richmond's 5-3 Game 1 loss to the Humboldt Broncos in what it describes as the Centennial Cup final best-of-seven series. The article was published April 22, 1987 — notably before the known May 9 Centennial Cup final — suggesting the competition referenced may be an earlier playoff round; editors should verify which series is actually described. Game 2 results were not available before the paper's press deadline.

before.descriptionShort: Abbotsford Pilots defeat expansion Richmond Sockeyes 8-7 in West Coast Junior Hockey League season-opener.
before.descriptionLong: The Abbotsford Pilots defeated the expansion Richmond Sockeyes 8-7 in a back-and-forth West Coast Junior Hockey League season-opener at MSA Arena. Scott Edwards' powerplay goal at 8:30 of the third period broke a 7-7 tie for Abbotsford. Richmond's Jason Talo recorded four points in the loss, while Todd Geddes earned offensive player of the game honours for the Pilots.
before.headline: Pilots Edge Sockeyes 8-7 in season-opener

after.descriptionShort: Abbotsford Pilots defeat expansion Richmond Sockeyes 8-7 in West Coast Junior Hockey League season-opener at MSA Arena.
after.descriptionLong: The Abbotsford Pilots defeated the expansion Richmond Sockeyes 8-7 in a back-and-forth West Coast Junior Hockey League season-opener at MSA Arena, before fewer than 200 fans. Scott Edwards' powerplay goal at 8:30 of the third period broke a 7-7 tie to give Abbotsford the win. Richmond's Jason Talo — a former BCJHL star and member of the 1987 Centennial Cup Sockeyes — recorded four points in the loss. Todd Geddes earned offensive player of the game honours for the Pilots for his playmaking and puck movement.
after.headline: Edge Sockeyes 8-7 in season-opener

before.descriptionShort: Coach Orland Kurtenbach discusses the Richmond Sockeyes' physical but disciplined style ahead of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series opener against the Red Deer Rustlers.
before.descriptionLong: Writing from Richmond, BC, Red Deer Advocate reporter Greg Meachem profiles Sockeyes general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach ahead of the opening game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series against the Red Deer Rustlers on April 7, 1987. Kurtenbach, a former hard-nosed NHL player with stops in New York, Toronto, and Vancouver, says he values physical but clean hockey with strong puck movement, telling the reporter "there's nothing wrong with good, tough hockey as long as it doesn't take away from the flow of the game." While acknowledging the BC-champion Sockeyes are "fairly aggressive overall," Kurtenbach emphasizes that excessive rough play — "goony stuff" — has no place in his system.
before.headline: Sockeyes Seek Tough Hockey in Centennial Cup Semifinal

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes devoured the Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in Game 1 of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal, extending their post-season unbeaten streak to 16 games.
before.descriptionLong: In the opening game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal at Richmond Arena on April 8, 1987, the Richmond Sockeyes overwhelmed the Red Deer Rustlers 11-4, extending their post-season unbeaten streak to 16 games before approximately 1,250 fans. Dave Tomlinson opened the scoring just 21 seconds in, and defenceman Matt Hervey quickly doubled the lead; Richmond fired 60 shots on Rustlers goaltender Glen Furgason while Frank Romeo faced only 34 shots and needed fewer than a half-dozen quality saves. Coach Orland Kurtenbach credited his team's relentless work ethic, while Rustlers coach Larry Billows lamented poor defence, weak goaltending, and a lack of intensity from his forwards. The article is illustrated by a Canadian Press photo of Rustler Randy Cave being contained near the Richmond goal by Stan Czenczek (#7) and Frank Romeo.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes dominate Red Deer Rustlers in Centennial Cup semifinal opener
before.byline: By Greg Meachem of The Advocate

before.descriptionShort: Red Deer Rustlers drop the first two games of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series to Richmond Sockeyes, including an 11-4 loss and a 6-4 defeat, but take confidence from their improved showing in game two.
before.descriptionLong: Writing from Richmond, BC, Greg Meachem of The Advocate reports that the Red Deer Rustlers lost the first two games of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series to the Richmond Sockeyes — an embarrassing 11-4 defeat in game one, followed by a 6-4 loss in game two. Despite the back-to-back losses, the Rustlers showed marked improvement in game two, leading 4-2 after two periods before the Sockeyes pulled away. The team returned home with confidence that they can compete against the large and mobile Sockeyes, who dominated the BCJHL playoffs before meeting Red Deer.
before.headline: Rustlers Take Confidence from Sockeyes Series
before.byline: By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Red Deer Rustlers 6-4 in Game 2 of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal, extending their post-season winning streak to 17 games and taking a 2-0 series lead.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes rallied from a 4-3 deficit with 13 minutes remaining in Game 2 of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal against the Red Deer Rustlers on April 8, 1987, at Richmond Arena, winning 6-4 to extend a post-season winning streak to 17 consecutive games. Richmond's comeback was sparked by a five-minute power play after Rustler defenceman Keith Lightbown was ejected for attacking Sockeye forward Dave Tomlinson, who never dropped his gloves; Dean Rutledge tied the game, Matt Hervey scored the winner (his hat-trick goal), and Brian Kozak added insurance during the man advantage. Despite strong goaltending from Red Deer pick-up Ken Weiss and a disciplined Rustler defence that had frustrated the Sockeyes through two periods, general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach's club pulled out the victory to lead the best-of-seven series 2-0, with all remaining games to be played in Red Deer.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup Semifinal Against Red Deer

before.descriptionLong: The Red Deer Advocate published the full series schedule, standings, and a detailed period-by-period summary of Wednesday's Centennial Cup West Semifinal game in which Richmond defeated Red Deer 6–4, extending their series lead to 2–0 (Tuesday's result was 11–4 for Richmond). Matt Hervey recorded a hat trick in Wednesday's game, with further goals from Steve Jaques, Dean Rutledge, and Brian Kozak; assists were distributed among Czenczek, Tomlinson, Rutherford, and McCormick. Goalies listed were Weiss (Red Deer) and Romeo (Richmond), with attendance of 800.

before.descriptionShort: CAHA warns the Alberta-B.C. junior championship between the Red Deer Rustlers and Richmond Sockeyes could be cancelled or moved unless fans stop throwing debris and eggs at players.
before.descriptionLong: The Canadian Amateur Hockey Association warned that unruly fan behaviour at Red Deer Arena has jeopardized the best-of-seven junior championship series between the Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes, tied 2-2 after Red Deer's 7-1 win in Game 4. The game was delayed 30 minutes by four on-ice battles and a fracas behind the Richmond bench in which disorderly fans threw debris including eggs — hitting one Richmond player in the face — along with tomatoes and fish thrown after every Rustler goal. The prior week in Richmond, two Red Deer players were suspended (one and three games) for a post-game altercation with fans, and the Richmond club was fined $500 by the CAHA for failing to provide adequate security.
before.headline: Alberta-B.C. junior hockey championship at risk due to fan unruly behavior
before.byline: By JACK WILSON of The Advocate

before.descriptionShort: Red Deer Rustlers post back-to-back wins over Richmond Sockeyes — 6-3 Saturday and 7-1 Sunday — to even their best-of-seven western Centennial Cup semifinal at 2-2, with Dave Tomlinson scoring Richmond's lone goal in the Sunday game.
before.descriptionLong: The Red Deer Rustlers swept a home weekend against the Richmond Sockeyes, winning 6-3 Saturday and 7-1 Sunday to deadlock their best-of-seven western Centennial Cup semifinal at 2-2, after Richmond had taken the first two games in B.C. 11-4 and 6-4, snapping the Sockeyes' 17-game winning streak. Rustlers goaltender Ken Weiss was outstanding, and Sunday's contest was marked by 392 penalty minutes — 221 to Richmond — including a 30-minute delay in the second period caused by four on-ice fights and a fan fracas behind the Richmond bench. Dave Tomlinson scored Richmond's only goal of Sunday's game, his fourth of the series, at 1:39 of the third period. Rustler coach Larry Billows noted that Richmond struggled with Red Deer's physical style, predicting the Sockeyes would have to earn any further wins before the series moved to Humboldt, site of the 1987 Centennial Cup.
before.headline: ALL RICHMOND SOCKEYES' CAN DO IS WATCH HELPLESSLY

before.descriptionLong: Weekend box scores from the Richmond Sockeyes' playoff series against the Red Deer Rebels, with both games played in Red Deer. On Saturday, April 11, Richmond lost 3-6, with goals from McCormick, Hardy (PP), and Tomlinson (SH); on Sunday, April 12, Richmond lost 1-7, with Richmond's lone goal from Tomlinson. Both game summaries include period-by-period scoring, goal scorers with assists, extensive penalty logs, and shots on goal; goaltenders were Romeo (Sat.) and Stewart (Sun.) for Richmond, and Weiss for Red Deer. before.descriptionShort: The Red Deer Rustlers defeated the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime to take a 3-2 series lead in the Western Centennial Cup semifinal, with Jason Phillips' late penalty shot stopped by Ken Weiss and Dixon Ward scoring the overtime winner 59 seconds into the extra period.
before.descriptionLong: The Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime on April 14, 1987, taking a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven Western Centennial Cup semifinal series. Dixon Ward scored the winner just 59 seconds into overtime, and the Rustlers sealed the game with an empty-net goal 14 seconds after Sockeyes forward Jason Phillips failed to convert a penalty shot — awarded after a Rustler player intentionally dislodged the goal — with 24 seconds left in OT. Goaltender Ken Weiss stopped 46 shots for Red Deer. Sockeyes GM-coach Orland Kurtenbach praised the refereeing in Game 5 while noting concerns about fan conduct in earlier games; Rustlers GM Graham Parsons credited Red Deer fans for heeding his appeal to stop throwing debris, after the CAHA had threatened to cancel or relocate the series.
before.headline: Rustlers Hold Off Sockeyes in Overtime

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 7-3 in Game 6 of the Western Centennial Cup semifinal, tying the best-of-seven series at 3-3 with the deciding game that evening.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 7-3 on April 15, 1987, before 2,020 fans at Red Deer Arena, tying the best-of-seven Western Centennial Cup semifinal series at 3-3. The deciding Game 7 was scheduled for that evening at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. Reporter Greg Meachem of The Advocate opens by characterizing the Rustlers' season as totally unpredictable — losing games they were expected to win and winning at unexpected times — framing their Game 6 collapse as consistent with that pattern.

before.descriptionLong: Box score and game summary from the Red Deer Advocate showing the Richmond Sockeyes defeating Red Deer 7–3 in the sixth game of the Doyle Cup best-of-seven series, leaving it tied 3–3 heading into a deciding Game 7 that night. Richmond exploded for five first-period goals, with Clarke recording a hat trick (including two power-play markers), and Bobbitt and Tomlinson also scoring; Red Deer managed just one first-period reply. The Sockeyes added goals from Kozak and McCormick in the third period to seal the win, while Red Deer's remaining goals came in the second period. Jamie Stewart was in goal for Richmond; attendance was 1,732.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 in Game 7 of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes eliminated the Red Deer Rustlers with a lopsided 9-1 victory in the seventh and final game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series on Thursday night. There were tears in the Rustlers' dressing room after the loss, according to reporter Greg Meachem of The Advocate.

before.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 in game seven of the western Centennial Cup tier-two junior hockey semifinal series on April 17, 1987, with Dave Tomlinson scoring three goals.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes eliminated the Red Deer Rustlers 9-1 in the seventh and deciding game of the western Centennial Cup tier-two junior hockey semifinal on April 17, 1987, at the Red Deer Arena. An exhausted Rustlers squad, playing their 27th post-season game in 47 days, could not match the bigger, more talented Sockeyes, who blew open a scoreless game with five unanswered second-period goals. Dave Tomlinson led the Sockeyes with three goals, while Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, and Jim Gunn also scored; Jamie Stewart was in goal for Richmond. Pete deGraaf scored the lone Red Deer goal late in the third period, and Sockeyes general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach praised his team's effort after the series win.
before.headline: Red Deer Rustlers Fall to Richmond Sockeyes in Centennial Cup Semifinal

before.descriptionShort: Dave Tomlinson's goal with 2:37 left gave the Richmond Sockeyes a 5-4 win over the Humboldt Broncos, claiming the Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey title four games to three.
before.descriptionLong: Dave Tomlinson scored 35 seconds after Humboldt tied the game to give the Richmond Sockeyes a 5-4 victory over the Humboldt Broncos on April 29, 1987, capturing the best-of-seven Western Canada Tier Two junior hockey title four games to three. Matt Hervey, Dean Rutledge, Jason Phillips, and Bill Hardy also scored for Richmond. The Sockeyes advance to the Centennial Cup tournament in Humboldt alongside host club Pembroke, Ont., and Dartmouth, N.S. The Broncos were booed by Richmond fans for skipping the post-game handshake, with Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch defiantly warning the Sockeyes they had not yet won anything.

before.descriptionShort: A Canadian Press round-robin recap from the 1987 Centennial Cup in Humboldt, SK, reporting that the Richmond Sockeyes beat Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3 and the Humboldt Broncos beat Pembroke 4-2 to take an early tournament lead.
before.descriptionLong: This Canadian Press game report from the 1987 Centennial Cup junior-A championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan covers two Sunday games: the Humboldt Broncos beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-2 to take an early round-robin lead (having also won the opener 7-2 over Dartmouth), while the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3. Jason Phillips scored three goals for Richmond, with Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey also scoring; the Sockeyes outshot Dartmouth 44-29 and led 3-1 after the first period and 6-2 after the second. Richmond was scheduled to face Pembroke in the only game on May 4.
before.headline: Early lead to Broncos

before.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeat Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play.
before.descriptionLong: Humboldt Broncos advanced to the Centennial Cup final with a 6-1 victory over Richmond Sockeyes on Wednesday in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Bill McDougall and Garnett Kazuik each scored twice for Humboldt, which finished the round-robin with a 3-0 record. Richmond, despite the loss, moved to a 2-1 record and qualified for the semifinal against Pembroke Lumber Kings, scheduled for that evening.
before.headline: Broncs in final

after.descriptionShort: Humboldt Broncos defeated Richmond Sockeyes 6-1 in Centennial Cup round-robin play, with Stan Czenczek scoring the lone Richmond goal.
after.descriptionLong: Humboldt Broncos clinched a spot in the Centennial Cup final with a 6-1 round-robin victory over the Richmond Sockeyes in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on Wednesday. Bill McDougall and Garnett Kazuik each scored twice for Humboldt, which finished the round-robin undefeated at 3-0. Stan Czenczek scored the only goal for Richmond, who finished 2-1 and moved on to face the Pembroke Lumber Kings in the semifinal that same evening, with the final set for Saturday.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in Centennial Cup semifinal to advance to championship final.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes advanced to the Centennial Cup junior-A championship final by defeating the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a semifinal game in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on Thursday night. Richmond's goals came from Trevor Dickie, Steve Dartmouth, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Bryon Moller, and Bill Hardy. The Sockeyes outshot Pembroke 44-32 and will face the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in the sudden-death final Saturday night.
before.headline: Sockeyes reach final

after.descriptionShort: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup semifinal in Humboldt, Saskatchewan, advancing to meet the Humboldt Broncos in Saturday's sudden-death final.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes (British Columbia) whipped the Ontario-champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Thursday-night semifinal at the Centennial Cup junior-A championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Goals came from Trevor Dickie, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Bryon Moller, and Bill Hardy; Richmond outshot Pembroke 44-32 and led 1-0 after one period and 3-0 after two. The Sockeyes will face the Saskatchewan-champion Humboldt Broncos — who went undefeated in round-robin play, including a 6-1 win over Richmond — in the sudden-death final Saturday night.
after.headline: Sockeyes reach final

Notable goaltenders include Glenn Raeburn from Ferris State College and Lyle Mast from Fort Saskatchewan. The team features six veteran defensemen including Jason Edgar, plus returning forwards Dan Baker and Tim Willoughby. Newcomers include defenseman Kevin Forsen from the Hobbema Hawks and winger Bill Hardy from the Richmond Sockeyes.

Other notable mentions include goaltender Glenn Raeburn (formerly of Ferris State College) and defenseman Kevin Forsen (Hobbema Hawks), while veteran defensemen Jason Edgar, Hall, Jenzen, Straub, Orion Donison, and Doug Gizoski are returning.

before.descriptionShort: Vernon Lakers edged the Coast Division-leading Richmond Sockeyes 7-6 in a BCJHL game Thursday night, January 29, 1987.
before.descriptionLong: Vernon's Doug Spooner fired four goals, with Dan Zeidel, Campbell Blair, and Kevin Scott also tallying, as the Lakers edged the Coast Division-leading Richmond Sockeyes 7-6 in a B.C. Junior Hockey League game Thursday night. Vernon led 4-2 after the first period and 6-4 after the second. Richmond's goals came from Dean Rutledge (2), Bill Hardy, Paul Rutherford, Jason Phillips, and Rob Clarke. The report, filed by The Canadian Press, also noted Merritt Warriors trounced the Summerland Buckaroos 8-2 in a separate game.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in game one of the best-of-seven Western semi-final between BC and Alberta Tier II junior hockey champions.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 on April 7, 1987, in game one of the best-of-seven Western semi-final between the BC and Alberta Tier II junior hockey champions. Bill Hardy, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey each scored twice, while Jim Gunn, Tony Bobbitt, Jason Phillips, Mike McCormick, and Dean Rutledge added single goals. Richmond led 4-2 after one period and 6-3 after two, outshooting Red Deer 60-34; game two was scheduled for that evening in Richmond before the series shifted to Red Deer.

before.descriptionShort: The Humboldt Broncos defeated the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 in overtime in the opening game of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final.
before.descriptionLong: The Humboldt Broncos scored twice in an overtime period to beat the Richmond Sockeyes 5-3 Monday night in the opening game of the best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western Canada junior hockey final in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. Curtis Chamberlain had three goals, including the overtime winner, for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos, with Brad Bergen and Bill McDougall adding the other Humboldt markers. The BC champion Sockeyes received goals from Bryon Moller, Matt Hervey, and Jason Phillips, and had led 2-1 after two periods before the teams were tied 3-3 after regulation.

before.descriptionShort: Mike Gaber's overtime goal gives Humboldt Broncos 4-3 win over Richmond Sockeyes in Centennial Cup playoff Game 6.
before.descriptionLong: Mike Gaber scored at 3:31 of overtime to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes on April 29, forcing a seventh deciding game in the Western Canada final. Bill McDougall scored twice for Humboldt while Paul Rutherford led Richmond with two goals. Humboldt's Bruce Hoffort made 42 saves as both teams were closely matched in shots, 46-45.

after.descriptionShort: Mike Gaber's overtime goal gives Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 win over Richmond Sockeyes in Centennial Cup Game 6, forcing a deciding Game 7.
after.descriptionLong: Mike Gaber scored at 3:31 of overtime to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes on April 29, forcing a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada Centennial Cup final. Paul Rutherford scored twice and Dave Tomlinson added the other Richmond goal, while Bill McDougall led Humboldt with two goals and Bruce Hoffort made 42 saves as the Broncos outshot the Sockeyes 46-45. Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach cited two key mistakes as the decisive factor in the loss.

before.descriptionShort: Jason Phillips scores three goals as Richmond Sockeyes defeat Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3 in Centennial Cup round-robin play on May 4, 1987.
before.descriptionLong: Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 7-3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in round-robin play at the Centennial Cup junior-A hockey tournament in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on May 4, 1987. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey also scored for Richmond, who piled up 44 shots to Dartmouth's 29. The Sockeyes jumped to a 3-1 lead after the first period and extended it to 6-2 in the second; it was Dartmouth's second loss in as many days, having previously fallen 7-2 to host Humboldt Broncos.
before.headline: Richmond Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 to go 2-0 in Centennial Cup round-robin play.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes won 4-1 over Pembroke Lumber Kings on Monday night at the Centennial Cup junior-A championship tournament in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. The Sockeyes' third-period explosion gave British Columbia's champions a 2-0 record and first-place tie with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. Dave Tomlinson scored twice, with Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek (winning goal) adding one each.
before.headline: Sockeyes win 2nd in Centennial

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1, exploding for three third-period goals to go 2-0 in Centennial Cup round-robin play in Humboldt, Saskatchewan.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes scored three goals in the third period to defeat the Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 on Monday night at the Centennial Cup junior-A championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan. The win gave BC's champions a 2-0 record and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. Dave Tomlinson led Richmond with two goals, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek each added one; Czenczek's winning goal came at 6:35 of the third period after the Sockeyes held a 1-0 lead through two frames. Richmond outshot Pembroke 26-25 but was assessed 11 of 15 minor penalties.
after.headline: Sockeyes win 2nd in Centennial

before.descriptionShort: University of Maine freshman defensemen Stan Czenczek and Dave Nonis look ahead to the 1985-86 season after the Bears' elimination from the Hockey East quarterfinals by Boston University.
before.descriptionLong: Following the University of Maine-Orono hockey team's elimination from the Hockey East quarterfinals by Boston University, freshman defensemen Stan Czenczek and Dave Nonis — both British Columbia natives — express optimism about the 1985-86 season. Both players acknowledge that freshman mistakes cost the team some games but credit the extensive playing time for their development. They express disappointment for the graduating senior class, who fell short of the Hockey East semifinals and finals in Providence, praising the seniors for supporting the younger players throughout the season.

before.descriptionShort: University of Maine freshman defenseman Stan Czenczek, a former BCJHL player from Surrey, BC, explains why he chose Maine over the University of Denver.
before.descriptionLong: University of Maine-Orono freshman defenseman Stan Czenczek, a 5-11, 185-pound Surrey, BC native who previously played in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League, recounts how he came "pretty close" to attending the University of Denver before choosing Maine. Czenczek credits Maine Coach Shawn Walsh's persistence — Walsh nearly abandoned his recruitment and had to learn of Czenczek's decision through Stan's sister Angie — as the decisive factor. Denver Coach Ralph Backstrom expressed disappointment, claiming his scholarship offer matched or exceeded Maine's in dollar value, but acknowledged Walsh "did a real good recruiting job" and that Stan had his heart set on Maine.
before.headline: Czenczek nearly opted for Denver

after.descriptionShort: University of Maine freshman defenseman Stan Czenczek, a former BCJHL player from Surrey, BC, explains why he chose Maine over the University of Denver.
after.descriptionLong: University of Maine-Orono freshman defenseman Stan Czenczek, a 5-11, 185-pound Surrey, BC native who previously played in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League, recounts how he came "pretty close" to attending the University of Denver before choosing Maine. Czenczek credits Maine Coach Shawn Walsh's persistence — Walsh nearly abandoned his recruitment and had to learn of Czenczek's decision through Stan's sister Angie — as the decisive factor. Denver Coach Ralph Backstrom expressed disappointment, claiming his scholarship offer matched or exceeded Maine's in dollar value, but acknowledged Walsh "did a real good recruiting job" and that Stan had his heart set on Maine.
after.headline: Czenczek nearly opted for Denver

issues: Czenczek is from Surrey, BC per the OCR, not Surrey — actually this matches, but the draft omits that he played in the BCJHL (British Columbia Junior Hockey League) before Maine, which is the direct Sockeyes-era league connection.

The draft says 'Denver Coach Ralph Backstrom acknowledged Maine's successful recruitment, noting he had matched Maine's scholarship offer' — this is misleading/partially inverted: Backstrom said he matched Maine's offer (or his offer was better in dollars), but Stan's heart was set on Maine. The draft implies Backstrom conceded Maine's offer was better, which is inaccurate.
The draft omits the key detail that Czenczek played in the British Columbia Junior Hockey League (BCJHL) before attending Maine — directly relevant to his Sockeyes-era background.
The draft omits the detail that Walsh nearly gave up on Czenczek and was headed to Chicago to recruit another defenseman before learning of Stan's decision through his sister Angie.

The draft's long description says Backstrom 'noted he had matched Maine's scholarship offer' — per OCR Backstrom said 'We matched Maine's offer. In fact, I thought our offer was better in terms of dollars and cents.' This nuance is missing/mischaracterized.

before.descriptionShort: Partial box score from a Richmond Sockeyes vs. Quesnel game, showing goals by Claringbull and Phillips and extensive penalty details for both sides.
before.descriptionLong: Two-period box score excerpt (periods 3 and 4) from a Sockeyes game against Quesnel, published in the Quesnel Cariboo Observer. Goals recorded for Richmond include Claringbull (assisted by Phillips and Kozak) and Phillips (assisted by Hardy and Czenczek). Numerous penalties were assessed to both teams across the two periods, including charging, high sticking, boarding, roughing, elbowing, slashing, interference, crosschecking, and unsportsmanlike conduct infractions involving Sockeyes players Kozak, Dickie, McCormick, Rutledge, Jaques, Claringbull, Czenczek, Gunn, and Moller.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in Centennial Cup semifinal to advance to championship final against Humboldt Broncos.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup junior-A hockey semifinal on May 7, 1987, to advance to the championship final. With a 2-1 round-robin record, Richmond will face Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in a sudden-death final on May 8. Goals were scored by Dickie, Jaques, Hervey, Kozak, Rutherford, Phillips, Bobbitt, Bryon, and Hardy. The Sockeyes led 1-0 after one period and 3-0 after two.
before.headline: Sockeyes Advance to Centennial Cup Final

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup junior-A semifinal on May 7, 1987, advancing to a sudden-death final against Humboldt Broncos on May 9.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Ontario champion Pembroke Lumber Kings 9-3 in a Centennial Cup junior-A hockey semifinal on May 7, 1987, advancing to the championship final. Richmond entered the semifinal with a 2-1 round-robin record and will face Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos in a sudden-death final on May 9. Goals were scored by Dickie, Steve Jaques, Matt Hervey, Brian Kozak, Paul Rutherford, Phillips, Tony Bobbitt, Bryon (Moller), and Bill Hardy; Pembroke replied through Dave Hoof, George Dupont, and Clarke. Richmond outshot Pembroke 44-32, took 11 of 17 penalties, and led 1-0 after one period and 3-0 after two.
after.headline: Sockeyes Advance to Centennial Cup Final

issues: Headline 'Sockeyes Advance to Centennial Cup Final' is not confirmed by the OCR — no headline text is present in the OCR excerpt, only body paragraph text.

The final is described as being on May 8 ('tomorrow night') but per the draft it says 'May 8'; the paper date is May 8 so the game was reported for May 9, not May 8 — the draft says the final is on May 8 which is the publication date, not the game date.

The draft says 'Goals were scored by Dickie, Jaques, Hervey, Kozak, Rutherford, Phillips, Bobbitt, Bryon, and Hardy' — the OCR lists 'Bryon' (likely Bryon Moller) separately, which is correct, but the first name 'Dickie' is listed without a first name in the OCR; the draft uses only 'Dickie' which is acceptable but note that 'Phillips' also has no first name in the OCR and is listed correctly.

Draft does not mention that Pembroke were the Ontario champions or that they had a 1-2 round-robin record, or that Pembroke scored through Dave Hoof, George Dupont, and Clarke — these are details from the OCR omitted from the description.
Draft does not mention Richmond outshot Pembroke 44-32 and took 11 of 17 penalties — notable game detail present in the OCR.
The date of the game was May 7 ('last night') per OCR, and the final is 'tomorrow night' (May 9), not May 8 as stated in the draft — the draft incorrectly states the final is on May 8.

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in the opening game of their best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western semifinal playoff series.
before.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes beat the Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 Tuesday night in Game 1 of their best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western semifinal, as reported by Canadian Press. Bill Hardy, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey each scored twice, while Jim Gunn, Tony Bobbitt, Jason Phillips, Mike McCormick, and Dean Rutledge also found the net. Richmond held period leads of 4-2 and 6-3, outshot Red Deer 60-34, and both teams scored three power-play goals while combining for over 190 minutes in penalties. Game 2 was scheduled for Richmond before the series shifted to Red Deer.
before.headline: Sockeyes crush Rustlers 11-4

after.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeated Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 in the opening game of their best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western semifinal playoff series.
after.descriptionLong: The Richmond Sockeyes beat the Red Deer Rustlers 11-4 Tuesday night in Game 1 of their best-of-seven Centennial Cup Western semifinal, as reported by Canadian Press. Game 2 was scheduled for Richmond before the series shifted to Red Deer.
after.headline: Sockeyes crush Rustlers 11-4

before.descriptionShort: Red Deer Rustlers rout the Richmond Sockeyes 7-1 in a chippy B.C.–Alberta Centennial Cup inter-league playoff game, evening the best-of-seven series at 2-2.
before.descriptionLong: In a chippy B.C.–Alberta Centennial Cup inter-league playoff game in Red Deer on April 12, 1987, the Red Deer Rustlers thumped the Richmond Sockeyes 7-1, evening the best-of-seven series at 2-2. Dixon Ward and Richard Anderson each scored twice for the AJHL champion Rustlers, with Brian Puhalsky, Tracy Georgsen, and Bob Duchak also scoring; Red Deer led 3-0 after the first period and 6-0 after the second, and outshot Richmond 46-34. Dave Tomlinson scored the Sockeyes' lone goal. The game was marked by heavy penalties — 28 minors, 20 majors, and 20 game misconducts — and the CP wire item notes that prior to this series, the Sockeyes had gone undefeated in playoffs, including a 2-0 sweep of Quesnel Millionaires (PCJHL champions).
before.headline: Red Deer Rustlers Defeat Richmond Sockeyes 7-1 in Centennial Cup Playoff

after.descriptionShort: Red Deer Rustlers rout the Richmond Sockeyes 7-1 in a chippy B.C.–Alberta Centennial Cup inter-league playoff game, evening the best-of-seven series at 2-2.

Dixon Ward and Richard Anderson each scored twice for the AJHL champion Rustlers, with Brian Puhalsky, Tracy Georgsen, and Bob Duchak also scoring; Red Deer led 3-0 after the first period and 6-0 after the second, and outshot Richmond 46-34. before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Humboldt Broncos 4-3 in overtime in Centennial Cup playoff game.
before.descriptionLong: Tony Bobbitt scored at 1:04 of overtime to give the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup junior-A playoff game. The victory gave Richmond a 2-1 lead in the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull, and Rob Clarke also scored for the Sockeyes, who were outshot 39-23 but rallied from a 2-1 deficit after two periods.
before.headline: Bobbitt's overtime goal gives Sockeyes Centennial Cup playoff win

after.descriptionShort: Tony Bobbitt scored 1:04 into a 10-minute overtime to give the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Humboldt Broncos, taking a 2-1 lead in the Centennial Cup Tier 2 western final series.
after.descriptionLong: Tony Bobbitt scored at 1:04 of a 10-minute overtime period to give the Richmond Sockeyes a 4-3 win over the Saskatchewan-champion Humboldt Broncos in a Centennial Cup junior hockey playoff game. The victory gave Richmond a 2-1 lead in the best-of-seven Tier 2 western final series, with Game 4 scheduled in Richmond on Saturday. Dave Tomlinson, Mike Claringbull, and Rob Clarke also scored for the Sockeyes, who were outshot 39-23 and trailed 1-0 after the first period and 2-1 after the second. Bill McDougall (two goals) and Curtis Chamberlin scored for Humboldt.
after.headline: Bobbitt's overtime goal gives Sockeyes Centennial Cup playoff win

The OCR clarifies Humboldt Broncos are the Saskatchewan champions, which is not mentioned in the draft.
The draft notes the overtime period without specifying it was a 10-minute overtime period, as stated in the OCR.

before.descriptionShort: AHL hockey results and NHL scores from Friday night, including Moncton Hawks' 8-2 victory over Cape Breton Oilers.
before.descriptionLong: Sports page reporting on AHL and NHL hockey games played Friday night. Alpo Suhonen, newly named Moncton Hawks coach and former Finland national team coach, earned his first AHL victory with an 8-2 win over Cape Breton Oilers. Suhonen addressed criticism from TV commentator Don Cherry regarding his hiring. Scores from other AHL games and multiple NHL matchups are also reported, including Toronto Leafs 10 Rangers 6, Buffalo Sabres 5 Penguins 1, and Winnipeg Jets 3 Devils 2.
before.headline: Suhonen gets first AHL win, brushes off Cherry criticism

after.descriptionShort: Former Richmond Sockeyes player Matt Hervey scored for the Moncton Hawks in an 8-2 AHL victory over the Cape Breton Oilers on February 17, 1989.
after.descriptionLong: Former Richmond Sockeyes player Matt Hervey is listed among the Moncton Hawks goalscorers in their 8-2 AHL victory over the Cape Breton Oilers. The game marked new Hawks coach Alpo Suhonen's first AHL win; Suhonen, formerly of Finland's national team, had been hired after Rick Bowness moved up to coach the NHL's Winnipeg Jets. The article also covers NHL results from Friday night, including Toronto Maple Leafs 10 Rangers 6, Buffalo Sabres 5 Penguins 1, and Winnipeg Jets 3 Devils 2 in overtime, as well as a separate sidebar on Don Cherry's controversial on-air remarks about Suhonen and the Jets organization.
after.headline: Suhonen gets first AHL win, brushes off Cherry criticism

before.descriptionShort: Winnipeg Jets GM Mike Smith addresses potential locker-room tension following Fredrik Olausson's contract dispute and attempted return to Sweden.
before.descriptionLong: Winnipeg Jets GM Mike Smith responded to brewing conflict within the team following Swedish player Fredrik Olausson's attempt to void his NHL contract and return to Färjestad in Sweden. Smith acknowledged that several players harboured resentment toward Olausson for allegedly quitting on the team, but insisted coaching staff would manage the situation. The article also covers prospects like centre McLlwain from North Bay, who impressed in camp with two goals and expressed determination to prove his value after limited ice time in Pittsburgh.
before.headline: Team unity goal for Jets despite Olausson tensions

after.descriptionShort: Winnipeg Jets training-camp coverage by Brian Smiley includes a brief mention of defenceman Matt Hervey (ex-Richmond Sockeyes) wrestling with Brent Hughes during a scrimmage in Saskatoon.
after.descriptionLong: A multi-part Winnipeg Jets training-camp column by Sun staff writer Brian Smiley, filed from Saskatoon, covers GM Mike Smith's response to Fredrik Olausson's contract dispute, contract signings for Randy Cunneyworth and Greg Paslawski, and camp battles involving prospects. Of Sockeyes-archive relevance, the column briefly notes that spirited winger Brent Hughes wrestled with defenceman Matt Hervey — both described as former Moncton teammates — during a scrimmage in which Hughes scored twice to pace his squad to a 5-3 win over Dale Hawerchuk's club.
after.headline: Team unity goal for Jets despite Olausson tensions

before.descriptionShort: Winnipeg Jets trades Pete Peters and Keith Acton to Philadelphia Flyers; Moe Mantha rejoins team via waivers.
before.descriptionLong: The Winnipeg Jets traded Pete Peters and Keith Acton to the Philadelphia Flyers on October 3, 1989, in a deal involving draft picks and coinciding with earlier waiver activity. Moe Mantha was claimed off waivers by the Jets, returning to the team after five years away. Goaltender Daniel Berthiaume was established as the Jets' No. 1 goalie following roster moves before the season opener against the New York Rangers.
before.headline: Peters, Acton sent down the 'Yellow Brick Road'

after.descriptionShort: A Winnipeg Sun article reporting that ex-Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey was among five players demoted to the Moncton Hawks as the Jets finalised their 1989-90 roster, alongside news of trades involving Pete Peeters and Keith Acton.
after.descriptionLong: A October 4, 1989 Winnipeg Sun story by Brian Smiley covers multiple Jets roster moves, most notably that former Richmond Sockeyes defenceman Matt Hervey was sent down to the AHL's Moncton Hawks along with Grant Richison, Bryan Marchment, goaltender Bob Essensa, and forward Brent Hughes. The Jets also traded goaltender Pete Peeters and forward Keith Acton back to the Philadelphia Flyers in exchange for draft picks, in a deal GM Mike Smith described as unrelated to waiver activity. Moe Mantha, claimed off waivers from Philadelphia, rejoined Winnipeg five years after being traded away, while Daniel Berthiaume was confirmed as the Jets' No. 1 goalie ahead of their season opener against the New York Rangers.
after.headline: Peters, Acton sent down the 'Yellow Brick Road'

issues: The draft's short and long descriptions entirely omit the Sockeyes-relevant content: Matt Hervey (1987 Richmond Sockeyes player) is named in the OCR as one of five players demoted to the Moncton Hawks, the Winnipeg Jets' AHL affiliate — this is the key archive reason this clipping was saved.

before.descriptionShort: Winnipeg Jets pre-season game article featuring goaltender Rick Tabaracci's demotion and rookie Shawn Cronin's physical play.
before.descriptionLong: The Winnipeg Sun reports on a Jets-Canucks pre-season game and roster moves. Goaltender Rick Tabaracci was reassigned to the farmclub Moncton Hawks after allowing 10 goals in two pre-season games. Rookie defenceman Shawn Cronin impressed with physical play despite skating limitations. The Jets tied Vancouver 3-3, with goals from Dale Hawerchuk, Matt Hervey, and Dave McIlwain.
before.headline: his dues

after.descriptionShort: Ex-Sockeyes player Matt Hervey scored a goal for the Winnipeg Jets in a 3-3 overtime pre-season tie against the Vancouver Canucks, as reported in The Winnipeg Sun.
after.descriptionLong: The Winnipeg Sun covers a Jets-Canucks pre-season game that ended in a 3-3 overtime tie, with goals for Winnipeg from Dale Hawerchuk, Dave McIlwain, and former Richmond Sockeyes player Matt Hervey. The article focuses primarily on goaltender Rick Tabaracci's demotion to the Moncton Hawks farmclub after allowing 10 goals in two pre-season games, and on rookie defenceman Shawn Cronin's physical play impressing the home crowd of 9,829 at the Arena. Daniel Berthiaume started in goal for Winnipeg, stopping 24 of 27 shots.
after.headline: his dues

before.descriptionShort: Richmond Sockeyes defeat Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3 in Centennial Cup championship game.
before.descriptionLong: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes dominated the Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3 to win the Centennial Cup junior-A hockey championship in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on May 3, 1987. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey also scored for Richmond, who outshot Dartmouth 44-29 and led 3-1 after the first period.
before.headline: Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup

after.descriptionShort: Jason Phillips scored three goals as the Richmond Sockeyes defeated the Dartmouth Fuel Kids 7-3 in a Centennial Cup round-robin game in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on May 3, 1987.
after.descriptionLong: Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to a 7-3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in a Centennial Cup junior-A round-robin game in Humboldt, Saskatchewan on May 3, 1987. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson, and Matt Hervey also scored for Richmond, who outshot Dartmouth 44-29 and led 3-1 after the first period and 6-2 after the second. It was Dartmouth's second loss in two days, having also fallen 7-2 to host Humboldt Broncos. Richmond was scheduled to face the Pembroke Lumber Kings (Ontario) in their next round-robin game.
after.headline: Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup

issues: The draft calls this the 'championship game' but the OCR clearly describes a round-robin tournament game, not the final. The Centennial Cup was a week-long round-robin format and this was just Richmond's first game.
The headline 'Sockeyes Win Centennial Cup' is fabricated — the OCR contains no such headline; this is a game report within the round-robin, not a championship-clinching win.

The long description says 'to win the Centennial Cup junior-A hockey championship' which is factually wrong — this was a round-robin pool game, not the final. Richmond went on to win the Cup on May 9.
The short description says 'championship game' which is incorrect for the same reason.

before.descriptionShort: Fort McMurray Oil Barons acquired left-winger Hendry from the Richmond Sockeyes (in a trade for Dean Rutledge), and in his two first games he scored twice in a Saturday 7-3 win and added a hat-trick in Sunday's game over the Hobbema Hawks.
before.descriptionLong: The Fort McMurray Oil Barons acquired left-winger Hendry from the Richmond Sockeyes in a deal for Dean Rutledge, and he made an immediate impact. In his first game on Saturday he scored twice in a 7-3 victory over the Hobbema Hawks, then followed up on Sunday by completing a hat-trick — including a backhand over the goalie for the final goal — in another Oil Barons win (leading 7-0 per the article). The Terrace, B.C. native, who had attended the NHL Hartford Whalers training camp the previous fall, impressed Oil Barons coach and GM Ivan Prediger with his play and hockey sense.
before.headline: Oil Barons 7, Hawks 3

after.descriptionShort: Fort McMurray Oil Barons acquired left-winger Hendry from the Richmond Sockeyes (in a trade for Dean Rutledge), and in his two first games he scored twice in a Saturday 7-3 win and added a hat-trick in Sunday's game over the Hobbema Hawks.
